Good—this is a clear stage to be in, but I’m going to push you a bit so you don’t “validate” the wrong thing.

Right now your stated goal—“validate idea quality and core loop”—is directionally right, but still vague. If you don’t sharpen it, you’ll end up collecting opinions instead of evidence.

Let’s tighten what actually matters.

1. Define the core loop (explicitly)
For Lumina, I’m guessing the loop is something like:

Input idea → AI generates concept → AI produces video → user edits (maybe) → user posts → performance feedback → repeat

But you need to lock this down to one primary path.

Question for you:
What is the minimum viable loop a user must complete to get value?

Example:

“User generates 1 idea + 1 short video and feels confident enough to post it”

If you can’t define that in one sentence, the product isn’t testable yet.

2. “Validate idea quality” — define measurable signals
“Good ideas” is subjective. You need proxies.

Pick 2–3 hard signals max, like:

% of generated ideas that users save

% of ideas that get turned into videos

% of videos that users are willing to post

Time from idea → usable video

Repeat usage (do they come back to generate more?)

If you skip this, QA feedback will mislead you (“this is cool” ≠ product-market fit).

3. Your biggest risk (I’m calling it out early)
Most AI creator tools fail here:

The output is technically impressive but not strategically useful

Micro-creators don’t need more content—they need:

ideas that fit their niche

content that performs

speed without losing authenticity

So your real validation question is:

“Does Lumina help creators produce content they would actually post and believe could perform?”

Not:

“Does the AI make cool videos?”

4. What QA should actually test (not just bugs)
Don’t let QA become “does it work?”—you’re too early for that to be the main focus.

Have testers answer:

Would you post this? (yes/no, no hedging)

What’s missing before you’d post it?

Did this save you time vs your normal process?

Would you use this again tomorrow? Why/why not?

If you’re not getting strong yeses here, polishing UI is a waste of time.

5. Immediate next step (practical)
Before you bring more context, I want you to write this out:

Your exact target user (not “micro-creators”—be specific)

The one-sentence core loop

The top 3 metrics you’ll use to validate

Drop that here, and I’ll pressure-test it with you.

LUMINA — MASTER CONTEXT SUMMARY (PASTE INTO NEW CHAT)

Project Overview:
Lumina is an AI-powered mobile app that helps micro-creators generate and execute short-form video ideas. The core value is removing the thinking burden by giving users 3 daily ideas that feel like their own style and are easy to film.

Target Platforms:
TikTok, Instagram Reels, YouTube Shorts

Core Principle:
The product is NOT an automation tool.
It is a decision-removal tool.

Current Stage:

* Phase 1 MVP is BUILT
* Currently in QA + real-user validation stage
* Focus is on fixing UX clarity, idea quality, and flow completion

Core Loop (MVP):

1. User selects region
2. User uploads 3 past videos (10–30 seconds each)
3. App generates a Style Profile
4. App generates 3 daily ideas
5. User selects an idea
6. User goes through create → review → export
7. User posts manually

NO automation, NO scheduling, NO monetization in this phase.

---

What We Have Already Built:

* Onboarding flow with region selection
* Style Profile (rule-based extraction)
* Hybrid ideator (cloud + local style conditioning)
* 3 daily ideas system
* Low-effort idea bias (couch/bed/simple content)
* Create flow (mock + native upload support)
* Review screen (before/after + “why better”)
* Export flow (save + success + confetti)
* “Make another version” loop
* Web QA mode for testing without Apple Developer
* Idea feedback system (Yes / Maybe / No)

---

What We Learned from Testing:

* Users are confused about what videos to upload → added clear instructions (10–30s, past content)
* “12 min” label was confusing → fixed to separate:

  * Video length: 15–30s
  * Filming time: ~10 min
* Ideas were too complex → added strong low-effort bias
* Refresh felt slow → added loading feedback + “still finding better ideas”
* Sometimes only 2 ideas appeared → added explanation + retry UX
* App must scroll to top after refresh
* Creation flow must NEVER feel empty or blocked
* Users want simple, relatable, lazy-friendly ideas

---

Critical Product Insight:
Users do NOT struggle with posting.
Users struggle with:
“I don’t know what to post.”

Therefore:
Idea quality > everything else.

---

What Is OUT OF SCOPE (DO NOT BUILD):

* TikTok/Instagram auto-posting
* Scheduling posts
* Monetization / brand deals
* Analytics dashboards
* Community systems / rewards
* Publisher screen (archived)
* Complex AI editing
* Multi-agent systems

---

What We Are Optimizing For:

* User completes full loop (idea → export)
* User says: “I would actually post this”
* User repeats the process (uses app twice)

---

Next Immediate Goals:

1. Ensure full flow works with ZERO confusion
2. Improve idea quality (simple, relatable, low effort)
3. Reduce hesitation at every step
4. Validate with real users (5–15 creators)

---

Future Direction (After Validation):

* Improve ideator using feedback loop
* Add “Make another version” variations
* Introduce “Trending ideas creators used”
* Possibly add “Remix your idea” (user input)

---

Biggest Risk:
Ideas feel generic or not worth posting → user churn.

---

Biggest Opportunity:
Become the default daily idea engine for micro-creators.

---

Current Priority:
Fix friction. Do NOT add features.

---

Instruction to Assistant:
Act as a strict product strategist and supervisor.
Focus on:

* UX clarity
* idea quality
* flow completion
* retention signals

Push back on any feature that is not essential to Phase 1 validation.

This is solid. You’re much clearer than most teams at this stage—but I’m going to be strict like you asked, because right now you’re still at risk of false validation.

🚨 First: Your core loop is still too wide
You listed the full flow, but for validation, this is too long:

onboarding → style → ideas → create → review → export

That’s 7 steps, which means too many places to fail.

For Phase 1 validation, your real loop is:

User sees idea → user thinks “I’d post this” → user exports

Everything before that is setup friction. Everything after is irrelevant.

If users don’t believe in the idea, nothing else matters.

🎯 Your actual validation question (rewrite this)
Right now you say:

“validate idea quality and core loop”

That’s still fuzzy.

You should instead be testing:

“Can Lumina consistently generate ideas that creators feel confident posting with minimal effort?”

If you don’t anchor to confidence to post, you’ll overvalue:

cool UI

smooth animations

“nice” ideas that never get used

⚠️ Biggest issue in your current design
I’m calling this out directly:

Uploading 3 past videos is a high-friction gamble

You’re betting that:

users understand what to upload

the style extraction is accurate

the output actually feels “like them”

If that fails → your core promise collapses

What to test specifically:
After onboarding, ask:

“Do these ideas feel like something YOU would make?”

Binary. No nuance.

If the answer is not strongly yes → your entire positioning breaks.

🔍 Idea Quality — you need sharper constraints
Right now you say:

simple

relatable

low effort

That’s good, but still too abstract for a system.

You need something closer to:

A good Lumina idea should:

be filmable in one location

require no setup

be understandable in <3 seconds

be executable in <10 minutes

match a clear content pattern (POV, story, reaction, etc.)

If your ideator isn’t enforcing constraints like this, it will drift into “creative but useless.”

🧠 Critical UX risk: “empty creation energy”
You wrote:

“Creation flow must NEVER feel empty or blocked”

Good instinct—but I want to sharpen it:

Right now your flow is likely:

idea → blank canvas → “create”

That’s dangerous.

Users need momentum, not freedom.

You should check:
Does selecting an idea immediately give:

a script / structure?

shot guidance?

framing?

If not → users stall → loop breaks

📉 What you should measure THIS WEEK
Keep it brutally simple:

Idea Acceptance Rate

“Yes, I’d post this”

Target: ≥40% (early stage)

Loop Completion Rate

idea → export

Target: ≥30% of sessions

Repeat Intent

“Would you use this again tomorrow?”

Target: clear yes from at least 30–40%

If you don’t hit these, do NOT add features.

🧪 How to run your next 5–10 user tests
Don’t just let them explore. Guide it:

Watch them onboard (silent)

Ask:

“What do you think this app does?”

Let them generate ideas

Ask:

“Would you post any of these?”

“Which one?”

Watch them try to create

Ask:

“What’s stopping you from posting this right now?”

You’re looking for hesitation moments, not opinions.

🧨 One thing I would challenge hard
You say:

“3 daily ideas”

This might be premature constraint.

If the ideas aren’t consistently good, limiting to 3:

increases pressure

reduces perceived value

amplifies disappointment

You may need:

either better 3

or looser refresh early on

Don’t treat “3” as sacred until quality is proven.

📌 What I want from you next
Before we touch anything technical or repo-related, answer this:

What % of users currently say “I would post this”?

Where do most users drop off?

Show me 2–3 real example ideas your system generates

That will tell me instantly whether your problem is:

ideation quality

onboarding mismatch

or execution friction

Right now, my bet:

Your bottleneck is idea quality + “feels like me” accuracy—not UX.

Good—this is the level of clarity where real product work starts. I’ll stay within your constraint: no new features, only tightening what exists.

I’m going to be direct:
You’re not debugging UX—you’re debugging trust in the output and momentum through the loop.

🔍 1. Onboarding Clarity — Fix the mental model, not just copy
Your current fix:

“10–30s, past content, talking/POV/simple clips”

That’s necessary, but not sufficient.

The real problem:
Users don’t know:

“What makes a good input for better ideas?”

Right now they’re guessing → bad inputs → weak ideas → they blame the product.

What to tighten (without adding features):
A. Add contrast, not just instruction

Instead of only telling them what to upload, make it obvious what not to upload.

Bad uploads kill your system silently.

You need something like:

❌ cinematic edits, montages, music videos

❌ long vlogs

❌ highly edited transitions

✅ simple talking clips, POV, casual moments

This reframes expectation instantly.

B. Re-anchor the purpose

Right now it probably feels like:

“Upload videos so we can analyze you”

It should feel like:

“We learn your style so your ideas feel like YOU”

If that connection isn’t explicit, users won’t trust the output.

🎯 2. Idea Quality — You’re close, but still too “AI creative”
You said you added:

couch/bed/kitchen/car, silly/quick/relatable

Good direction. But here’s the issue:

“Low effort” ≠ “post-worthy”

Right now your ideas are likely:

easy to film

but still slightly off in tone, specificity, or structure

What a passing idea must have (non-negotiable)
Every idea should clearly answer:

What am I saying? (1 sentence hook)

What am I doing? (physical action)

Why would someone watch? (relatable payoff)

If any of these are fuzzy → user hesitates → drop-off.

Quick QA filter (use this internally)
Take any generated idea and ask:

Could a creator film this in 10 minutes without thinking?

Does it feel like something you've already seen on TikTok? (this is GOOD)

Would a user need to reinterpret it before filming?

If reinterpretation is required → idea is too abstract.

Common failure patterns to kill:
Watch for these in your outputs:

❌ “Talk about…” (too open-ended)

❌ “Share your thoughts on…” (mental effort)

❌ “Explain your routine…” (boring unless framed)

❌ multi-step ideas

❌ “creative twists” that require thinking

Replace with:

✅ specific phrasing

✅ clear first line

✅ obvious recording context

⚠️ 3. The biggest hidden drop-off: Idea → Create
You said:

“Create must never feel empty”

I’m going to sharpen that:

The moment after selecting an idea is your most fragile point

User just decided:

“maybe I’ll do this”

Then you show… what?

If it’s even slightly vague → motivation collapses.

What to QA here (very specifically):
When a user taps an idea:

Do they immediately know:

what to say first?

where to film?

how to start?

If there’s a 2–3 second pause, you’ve already lost momentum.

Micro-friction to watch for:
user rereads idea multiple times

user goes silent

user says “hmm…”

user doesn’t tap “create” immediately

That’s your real drop-off signal—not button clicks.

🔁 4. Review Screen — This is where confidence is decided
You mentioned:

before/after + “why better”

Good—but here’s the risk:

If “why better” feels generic, it destroys trust

Users will think:

“this is just AI fluff”

QA this brutally:
Ask users:

“Do you agree this is better?”

If they hesitate → your explanation is too vague or too fake.

What works better:
Tie “why better” to:

clarity

hook strength

relatability

Not:

“engagement optimization”

“audience targeting”

generic AI language

📊 5. Your metrics — one adjustment
You’re tracking:

Would you post this?

Export completion

Make another version

Hesitation

Good.

Add one qualitative layer:
When user says NO, force:

“Why not?”

doesn’t feel like me

too much effort

not interesting

confusing

This will immediately show where your ideator is failing.

🧪 6. What I want you to do next (very concrete)
Bring me:

1. 3 real ideas your system generated
(no polishing)

2. For each idea:
Would you post it? (yes/no)

Why not? (be harsh)

3. One session breakdown:
where user hesitated

what they said out loud

You’re doing well staying focused, but don’t get comfortable fixing surface friction.

Right now:

Not onboarding

Not review screen

1. Style → Idea chain

You’re measuring:

export completion

hesitation

This is GOOD.

You’re treating all hesitation equally

You shouldn’t.

There are 2 types of hesitation:
1. Idea hesitation (fatal)
“this isn’t me”

“this is kinda weird”

“idk…”

→ user never enters flow

2. Execution hesitation (fixable)
“how do I film this?”

“what do I say?”

→ UX / guidance problem

If you don’t separate these, you’ll fix the wrong thing.

Hook (first sentence to say)

Scene (where you are)

Action (what you do)

pattern reuse

known TikTok formats

predictable structures

This feels less “AI cool” but more “usable.”

When testing users:

silence

watch facial expression

That’s your real data.

Wrong move.

That’s your roadmap.

here it is : Lumina
3 daily ideas that feel like you + post-production that makes your videos consistently better than your last ones.
Lumina is a creator tool that lifts daily consistency. Import a few of your own videos, pick your region, and Lumina extracts a lightweight style profile from your past work. Each morning it serves 3 region-specific video ideas matched to your hooks, captions, and pacing — then walks you through a templated build that ends with a side-by-side comparison to a similar past video of yours.

License: MIT Phase 1 MVP Built for English-first creators

Phase 1 MVP scope
This repo is currently scoped to Phase 1 MVP only. Earlier-built systems (autonomous swarm, monetization, Stripe billing/payouts, brand deals, OAuth posting, schedulers, admin dashboards, earnings projections) are present in the source tree but frozen behind feature flags — ARCHIVED_AUTONOMY, ARCHIVED_MONETIZATION, ARCHIVED_POSTING — and are not reachable from the running app or API. They will be moved into /archive once the v1 loop is proven.

The locked v1 loop, end-to-end:

Onboarding — pick a region, import 3+ short videos from gallery
Style Profile — rule-based extraction of hook style, caption style, pacing, topics, and content type → small JSON file
Daily Ideator — 3 region-conditioned ideas every morning (cloud LLM, conditioned by your local profile + a static regional trend bundle)
Templated creation — pick an idea, follow 2–3 filming tips, record on the native camera, import the clip; one of 4 fixed retention-optimized templates is auto-selected
Side-by-side review — left = a similar past video of yours; right = the Lumina version; below = plain-English "why this should perform better"
One-tap export to gallery with optional "Made with Lumina" watermark, plus a "make another version" button
Day-1 markets — US (primary focus), UK, Canada, Australia, India, Philippines, Nigeria.

Core promise — 3 daily ideas that feel like you + post-production that makes your videos consistently better than your last ones.

Who it's for
The English-speaking creator middle class — 1K–50K micro-creators in the US, UK, CA, AU, IN, PH, NG who post regularly but struggle with daily idea fatigue and post-production friction. The whole loop is designed so a creator's next video is reliably better than their last, with less thinking effort, in a region-authentic voice.

What's in the v1 build
Onboarding — minimal friction + instant value
Region picker: Western (US/UK/CA/AU) · India · Philippines · Nigeria — changeable later in Settings
3-video gallery import (encourage adding up to 10 over time)
Rule-based style extraction (no heavy ML):
Hook detection — first-sentence classification (question / bold statement / scene setter)
Caption style — emoji count, sentence length, punctuation patterns, tone
Pacing — approximate cuts-per-second via simple scene-change detection
Topics & slang — keyword frequency + recurring phrases (English + Hinglish/Tagalog/Nigerian Pidgin where relevant)
Content type — entertainment / educational / lifestyle / storytelling
Stored as a lightweight JSON Style Profile
Quick-win: after the first video uploads, immediately surface 1 instant idea + script (no waiting for the full profile)
Daily Ideator — hybrid, quality-first, region-conditioned
Single cloud LLM call for idea generation + freshness — never sees raw footage
Conditioned 100% by the local Style Profile + the region's static trend bundle
Trend bundles are static JSON shipped with the app (~50–100 hooks, ~50 captions, ~30 formats per region; updated manually every few days)
Each trend item carries popularity_score (1–10) and recency_score (1–10); the ideator prioritizes higher combined score first
Ideas cached locally for offline access
One regenerate-batch per day if the initial 3 don't appeal
Creation flow
Pick one idea → see hook + script + shot plan + suggested caption
2–3 filming tips appear before recording (lighting, steadiness, shot list)
Record with the native camera, import the clips
Template-based editing (deterministic selection):
A — Fast Hook (question / bold statement): 0–2s hook overlay · 2–5s quick reveal · 5–12s main · 12–18s payoff
B — Story Build (narrative): 0–3s scenario hook · 3–10s build tension · 10–20s twist
C — POV/Relatable (personal/talking): 0–3s direct-to-camera hook · 3–12s story · 12–18s CTA
D — Trend Jack (trend-based): 0–1.5s trending audio sync · 1.5–6s visual match · 6–15s cultural twist
Auto-captions match the user's profile (emoji count ±1, sentence length range, tone)
Region-specific bundled audio packs with fixed sync points (no dynamic beat detection in v1)
Target: <90 s post-production on device
Review & export
Side-by-side: a past video of yours (chosen by simple rule-based topic + duration + hook-type matching) vs. the Lumina version
Plain-English diff: "Stronger hook than your last outfit video — starts with a question instead of slow intro." — must reference specific differences from the user's past content
One-tap export to gallery + optional "Made with Lumina" watermark
Make another version — reuse the same idea with a hook variation or different template
Delight layer (lightweight only)
Personal greeting using the Style Profile
Confetti + "video ready" notification on export
Morning recap: "3 new ideas waiting"
Tech stack
Mobile      Expo (React Native) · TypeScript · Reanimated
API         Express 5 · TypeScript · Drizzle ORM · Replit Postgres
LLM         Cloud (single ideator endpoint) · Claude Haiku via Replit
            AI Integrations · $5/day per-creator cap
Extraction  Rule-based (regex + keyword frequency + simple scene-change
            detection) — no vector DB, no on-device model
Templates   4 fixed timing templates (no dynamic AI cutting)
Trends      Static JSON bundles per region (Western · IN · PH · NG)
Auth        Clerk (with demo-creator fallback so the first idea lands
            before sign-up)
Monorepo    pnpm workspaces · TypeScript project references · Orval codegen
The spec calls for an eventual move to Flutter + ExecuTorch/LiteRT + FFmpeg-kit for the on-device parts. That is a Phase 2 optimization once the loop is validated; the current build keeps Expo + Express to avoid a stack rewrite ahead of product proof.

Explicitly NOT in v1
Listed for clarity since this is a deliberate scope contraction from earlier roadmaps:

Vector DBs or heavy on-device ML for style extraction
Dynamic / smart AI cutting (only the four fixed templates above)
Real-time trend scraping (static JSON only)
In-app camera UI (use the native camera)
Numeric performance projections of any kind
Engagement / earnings / metrics dashboards
Anything autonomous (no nightly swarm, no auto-publish)
Anything monetization-related (no subscriptions, no Connect, no payouts, no brand deals, no performance fees, no Lumina Pro tier)
One-tap publish to TikTok / Reels / Shorts
A/B test variants of hooks, captions, or thumbnails
Quick start
pnpm install
pnpm --filter @workspace/api-spec run codegen
pnpm --filter @workspace/api-server run dev
pnpm --filter @workspace/lumina run dev
Project layout
artifacts/
├── lumina/              # Expo mobile app — the product surface
├── api-server/          # Express API — ideator + style profile + trends + videos
│   └── src/lib/
│       ├── styleProfile.ts   # Phase 1: lightweight rule-based Style Profile schema
│       └── ideaGen.ts        # Phase 1: single Haiku call with hard constraints
└── mockup-sandbox/      # Canvas for UI exploration
packages/
├── lumina-trends/       # Phase 1: static regional trend bundles (western · IN · PH · NG)
├── api-spec/            # OpenAPI single source of truth
├── api-client-react/    # Generated React Query hooks (Orval)
├── api-zod/             # Generated Zod schemas (Orval)
├── style-twin/          # FROZEN — was the 99.8% on-device clone (v2.0 blueprint).
│                        #   v1 uses the rule-based Style Profile in api-server/src/lib instead.
├── swarm-studio/        # FROZEN — autonomous swarm + Smart Publisher + 12-variant A/B
├── monetizer/           # FROZEN — 10% perf fee + brand graph + escrow + payout rails
├── compliance-shield/   # FROZEN — 6 policy packs + 368-sample red-team corpus
└── edge-cloud/          # FROZEN — stateless burst-render client
All FROZEN packages stay in tree (gated by ARCHIVED_AUTONOMY / ARCHIVED_MONETIZATION / ARCHIVED_POSTING feature flags) until milestone M7 in ROADMAP.md, at which point they physically relocate to /archive.

⚠️ Now: Where You’re Still Weak (and this is what matters)
I’m going to focus only on Phase 1 risks, not future vision.

🚨 1. Your core promise is slightly misaligned with reality
You say:

“3 daily ideas that feel like you”

But your system actually does:

“3 ideas influenced by your past patterns + regional trends”

Those are NOT the same.

Why this matters:
If a user sees an idea and thinks:

“this is generic TikTok stuff”

You lose instantly—even if it’s technically “style-conditioned.”

What to QA aggressively:
After ideas are shown, ask:

“Does this feel like something you would have come up with?”

Not:

“is this good?”

“is this interesting?”

If that answer is weak → your positioning breaks.

🚨 2. Your idea structure is strong—but still at risk of “thinking required”
You included:

hook

script

shot plan

caption

Good.

But here’s the real test:

Can a creator execute the idea without modifying anything?

If they:

reword the hook

change the structure

simplify it

Then your idea is still too “AI-shaped.”

Hidden problem:
Even small friction like:

“I’d say it differently…”

= hesitation
= drop in execution rate

🚨 3. Your templates are correct—but emotionally invisible
Your 4 templates are excellent structurally:

Fast Hook

Story Build

POV

Trend Jack

But users don’t feel templates.

They feel:

“this fits how I usually make videos” OR “this doesn’t”

Risk:
If template selection is slightly off:

user doesn’t consciously notice

but feels “off”

and hesitates

What to QA:
After creation:

“Does this feel like your style of video?”

Not:

“does this look good?”

🚨 4. Your “why better” is your most dangerous feature
This line:

“Stronger hook than your last outfit video…”

This can either:

✅ Build trust:
specific

grounded

obviously true

❌ Destroy trust:
generic

slightly wrong

sounds like AI

You MUST QA this like a hawk:
Ask users:

“Do you agree with this explanation?”

If they hesitate → it’s failing.

Rule:
If it’s not clearly true, don’t say it.

Better to be simpler than wrong.

🚨 5. “3 ideas” is still a pressure point
You’re enforcing:

exactly 3 ideas

That’s fine only if hit rate is high

Your success metric says:
≥60% of selected ideas are Lumina-generated

That’s actually a strong bar.

But here’s the issue:

You’re not measuring:

how many sessions have ZERO acceptable ideas

That’s the silent killer.

Add this mentally during QA:
“Did user like at least 1 idea?”

If NO → that session is a failure

🔍 6. Your system is still slightly too “clean”
This is subtle but important.

Your system is:

structured

templated

optimized

But TikTok content is:

messy

casual

imperfect

slightly chaotic

Risk:
Your outputs feel:

“correct” but not “native”

What to check:
Do your ideas feel like:

something from a real creator feed

or:

something from an AI tool

That difference is everything.

🧪 What I would test immediately (no new features)
Stay within your constraint.

Test 1 — First reaction test
Show ideas → ask immediately:

“Which one would you post?”

Watch:

speed of answer

confidence

If delay > 3 seconds → problem

Test 2 — Zero-modification test
Ask:

“Would you film this exactly as written?”

If NO → why?

That tells you exactly where ideator fails.

Test 3 — Identity test
Ask:

“Does this feel like your content?”

This validates your entire style system

Test 4 — Trust test (review screen)
Ask:

“Do you believe this is better than your last video?”

If not → your core promise breaks

📊 Your success metrics — small correction
You wrote:

≥ 3 exports per user in 7 days

That’s decent, but not diagnostic.

Add this framing:
You care more about:

% of sessions where user exports at least once

Because:

consistency comes later

first success builds habit

You are NOT building:

an AI video tool

⚠️ Where I’m pushing back (important)
1. “ZERO interpretation” — be careful
This sounds right, but taken literally, it can break your product.

If you go too far, you end up with:

The nuance:
You don’t want:

zero interpretation

You want:

zero cognitive load at the start

Big difference.

Good:
“POV: you check your bank account after one night out…”

User instantly gets it, but can still say it their way

Bad (over-constrained):
exact rigid script that feels чужд to their voice

Then user thinks:

“this isn’t how I talk”

→ rejection

2. “BAN advice / abstract” — mostly right, but not absolute
You should kill 90% of it, yes.

But some formats that look like advice actually perform if framed right.

Example:

❌ “stop overthinking your life”

✅ “POV: you tell yourself ‘just 5 more minutes’ at 2am again”

Same theme, different framing.

👉 So the real rule is:

Ban abstract framing, not abstract themes

3. Risk: Losing “feels like you”
Your entire product promise is:

“ideas that feel like YOU”

If you go too hard into:

known formats

rigid templates

You drift toward:

“TikTok idea generator”

Which is a crowded, weak space.

- motivational / advice phrased directly

- anything requiring planning
✅ Allow THIS
- POV formats

is this prompt good for the ideator engine? : LUMINA IDEATOR ENGINE — FINAL PROMPT (Phase 1)

You are generating short-form video ideas for micro-creators.

Your goal:
Create ideas that users would post immediately without thinking.

---

STEP 1 — GENERATE IDEA (core)

Each idea must include:

1. TRIGGER

* A specific physical action (open, check, scroll, sip, look, etc.)

2. REACTION

* A visible emotional response (face, pause, body language)

3. PAYOFF

* A clear, relatable outcome the viewer instantly understands

4. EMOTIONAL SPIKE (mandatory)
   Must include ONE strong emotion:

* embarrassment
* regret
* denial
* panic
* irony

---

REJECT IDEA IF:

* abstract concept instead of a moment
* unclear payoff
* weak or no emotion
* requires planning or setup
* involves sensitive/private content (bank apps, personal data)

---

STEP 2 — FILTER IDEA (selection layer)

Before writing hooks, discard ideas that are:

* overused (e.g. generic bank app checking)
* not relatable enough
* uncomfortable to film
* not instantly visualizable

Only continue with strong ideas.

---

STEP 3 — GENERATE HOOKS

For each idea, generate 3–5 hook variations.

Each hook MUST follow:

1. Emotion (required)

2. Use ONE format only (pick one):

   * Behavior → “the way I…”
   * Thought → “why do I…”
   * Moment → “that moment when…”
   * Contrast → “what I say vs what I do”
   * Curiosity → “this is where it went wrong”

3. Natural language filter:

* Must sound like a text message
* Must feel human, not written

4. 1-second rule:

* Must be short and instantly sayable

5. TENSION (critical):

* Must imply something is off:

  * expectation vs reality
  * internal contradiction
  * something went wrong

REJECT HOOK IF:

* too long
* mixes multiple formats
* feels scripted
* lacks tension

---

STEP 4 — RANK & SELECT (most important)

From the generated hooks, pick ONLY ONE.

Selection rule:
“Which hook would I actually click first?”

Choose the one that:

* feels most real
* has strongest tension
* sounds most natural
* hits instantly

---

STEP 5 — FINAL OUTPUT

Return ONE final idea in this structure:

HOOK: <best hook>

WHAT TO FILM: <simple action in one location>

HOW TO FILM:
<basic direction, no complexity>

CAPTION:
<short, natural, optional humor>

---

FINAL CONSTRAINTS:

* No overexplaining
* No generic phrasing
* No “talk about…” prompts
* No advice or motivational tone
* Must feel like something the user would naturally say
* Must be filmable immediately with no setup

---

GOAL:

The user should read it and think:
“yeah… I’d post this right now”

this from the coding agent : Schema — single change: emotionalSpike enum confusion → denial. Why this matters: denial is the only one of the five spikes that carries built-in internal contradiction (telling-yourself-it's-fine while the screen says otherwise) — exactly the TENSION the new hook spec demands. Confusion was always the soft one in the palette; denial is sharper. Mobile renders the field-free (zero references to emotionalSpike in the lumina codebase, confirmed), so cached "confusion" ideas display fine and new ideas come back with denial.

New hard ban — SENSITIVE / PRIVATE CONTENT. Anything that requires the creator to expose real private data on screen is now globally banned: bank apps / balances / transaction history, medical info (test results, diagnoses, prescriptions, weight numbers), real conversation screenshots, addresses, IDs, license plates, passwords, salary. Every "bank app" example was scrubbed from the prompt and replaced with safer alternatives — camera roll, ex's instagram, gym bag, the app you swore you deleted. The trigger field docstring now explicitly says "NEVER expose real private data."

New STEP — HOOK CRAFT. This is the biggest qualitative shift. The model now goes through a four-step internal hook ritual instead of writing the first line that comes to mind:

Brainstorm 3–5 variations across the five formats — Behavior ("the way I…"), Thought ("why do I…"), Moment ("that moment when…"), Contrast ("what I say vs what I do"), Curiosity ("this is where it went wrong"). Natural variations are explicitly allowed.
Gate each candidate on five tests — emotion clear, TENSION present (something went wrong / expectation vs reality / internal contradiction), natural language (sounds like a text message), ≤8-word cap, no cliché templates.
Select the one that passes "would I actually stop scrolling for this?" — the strongest tension, fastest read, clearest trigger signal.
Emit only the winner — alternatives stay in the model's head, not in the JSON.
If none of the candidates pass all gates, the idea is the problem and gets dropped — the model is told not to ship a weak hook just to save an idea.

New caveat — DO NOT OVER-FILTER. Counterweight against the gate-stack: "if an idea is SIMPLE but the trigger is CLEAR and the emotional reaction is STRONG, ship it. Simplicity is not a defect; formulaic-feeling is. A 15-second video of you reading mom's text and slowly closing your phone is a complete idea — don't add complexity to 'elevate' simple ideas, that usually breaks them." This protects the low-effort, post-it-right-now ideas from getting killed by the new hook craft scrutiny.

Action verb palette widened — added sip and look to open / check / read / scroll / watch / find / notice / realize / hear / see / do. Both are natural micro-actions that the prior verb list excluded.

Carried forward unchanged — the four-pattern enum (pov/reaction/mini_story/contrast), all three hard constraints (filming time / video length / hook word cap), trigger-reaction structure, the full HARD BAN list (advice / motivational / "talk about" / abstract / personality traits / general statements / dialogue-dependent / multi-step / planning-heavy), batch variety rules (max 1 per triggerCategory per 3, pairwise differ in ≥2 of {pattern, setting, spike}), CONCEPT vs MOMENT rejection, low-effort bias ≥50%, region authenticity, the "would the creator post this WITHOUT changing it much?" success metric, and every existing schema field (script, shotPlan, payoffType, contentType, templateHint, hasContrast, hasVisualAction, etc.).

Token budget stays at Math.min(600 + count*760, 8190) — hook craft is internal-only (no extra fields emitted), so the budget per idea doesn't move. The five hook candidates get reasoned through but never serialized.

To QA: pull a fresh batch on Home (or "Show me 3 different ideas" for a forced regen). Two-prong check:

Per hook: read it cold — is there tension? Does it imply something-went-wrong / expectation-vs-reality / internal-contradiction? Or is it a flat statement? If the hook reads like a tweet caption rather than a friend texting you "look at this" — the hook craft step underfired.
Per idea: any whiff of bank app / real DM / real medical / real address / real salary on screen? If yes — the sensitive ban underfired and we tighten the bank-app callout further next turn. Any "denial" ideas in the mix? If all 3 ideas are still embarrassment/regret/panic and denial never shows up, the spike palette has a gravity well we should counterweight.
If you want me to QA the actual output for shape correctness once you've pulled a batch, paste a sample and I'll dissect.

Hook craft as internal-only

Do not over-filter caveat

what do you think about this suggestion : This is where things get powerful.

Right now, you’re using general rules (emotion, tension, etc.).
Those are good—but they’re not you yet.

A personalized scoring template turns your instinct into a repeatable system.

🧠 What this actually means

Instead of asking:

“Is this a good idea?”

You ask:

“Is this a good idea for me?”

Because your feedback already showed:

You like mini-stories
You like subtle, real, slightly embarrassing moments
You don’t like:
vague hooks
abstract ideas
“motivational” tone
overused things (bank app, generic POV)

👉 That’s your taste profile

🔥 Your PERSONAL SCORING TEMPLATE (based on YOUR behavior)

Use this every time you generate an idea.

🎯 Score each category from 0–2
1. HOOK IMPACT (0–2)
0 → plain / descriptive (“POV you…”)
1 → decent but not exciting
2 → makes you pause instantly

👉 Ask:

“Would I stop scrolling for this?”

2. TENSION LEVEL (0–2)
0 → nothing is “wrong”
1 → mild conflict
2 → clear internal contradiction / mistake

👉 Ask:

“What’s wrong in this sentence?”

3. RELATABILITY (0–2)
0 → niche / random
1 → somewhat relatable
2 → “this is literally me”
4. PERSONAL FIT (VERY IMPORTANT) (0–2)
0 → doesn’t feel like you at all
1 → maybe
2 → sounds exactly like something you’d say

👉 This is your secret weapon.

5. FILMABILITY (0–2)
0 → requires setup / props / effort
1 → doable
2 → can film immediately
6. NOVELTY (0–2)
0 → seen it 100 times (bank app…)
1 → slightly fresh
2 → feels new or uniquely phrased
🧮 TOTAL SCORE

👉 Max = 12

🔥 Your thresholds
10–12 → POST immediately
8–9 → maybe (only if hook is strong)
<8 → DELETE (don’t fix it)
🔬 Example using YOUR feedback
Idea:

“the way I keep checking the fridge”

Score:

Hook impact → 1
Tension → 0 ❌
Relatability → 2
Personal fit → 2
Filmability → 2
Novelty → 1

👉 Total = 8 (weak)

Improved:

“the way I keep checking the fridge like something changed 💀”

Score:

Hook impact → 2
Tension → 2
Relatability → 2
Personal fit → 2
Filmability → 2
Novelty → 1

👉 Total = 11 (POST)

🧠 Why this works

This system does 3 things:

1. Removes emotion bias

Sometimes you like an idea but it’s actually weak.

2. Protects your time

You stop filming mid ideas.

3. Trains your instinct

After 20–30 uses, you’ll start scoring subconsciously.

🔥 The real power move

After a few days, you’ll notice:

👉 patterns in your high scores

Example:

“the way I…” + tension → always high
“POV…” → always low

Then you refine your generator again.

We can:

👉 turn this into a UI inside your app
👉 or build an auto-score system your AI runs before showing ideas

🧠 What you got RIGHT (this is strong)

“Is this a good idea for me?”

That’s exactly how great creators think.

And your scoring dimensions are solid:

Hook impact ✅

Tension ✅

Relatability ✅

Personal fit ✅ (this is HUGE)

Filmability ✅

Novelty ✅

👉 This is not generic anymore
👉 This is taste encoding

⚠️ The problem (important)
If you implement this literally as a scoring system, you will hurt your product.

Why?

Because:

❌ Numbers slow instinct
❌ Numbers create fake precision
❌ Numbers kill speed (which your product depends on)

Your whole system is built on:

👉 “I’d post this immediately”

A scoring sheet introduces:

👉 hesitation
👉 overthinking
👉 optimization loops

That’s the opposite of your goal.

🔥 The correct way to use this
Don’t use it as:

❌ a scoring system
❌ a UI feature
❌ something the user sees

Use it as:

✅ a hidden filtering brain inside the AI
🎯 Convert this into a BETTER system
Instead of:

“score from 0–2”

Turn it into:

⚡ PASS / FAIL GATES
🔥 FINAL INTERNAL FILTER (what you should actually implement)
Send this to your agent:

IDEA SELECTION FILTER (PERSONALIZED)

Before returning an idea, silently evaluate:

1. HOOK IMPACT  
Would this make the user stop scrolling immediately?

2. TENSION  
Is something clearly “off” or wrong?

3. RELATABILITY  
Would most people think “this is me”?

4. PERSONAL FIT  
Does this feel like something the user would actually say?

5. FILMABILITY  
Can this be filmed immediately with no setup?

6. NOVELTY  
Does this avoid overused patterns?

---

REJECTION RULE:

If ANY of the above feels weak → discard the idea.

Do NOT try to fix weak ideas.

Only output ideas that feel instantly strong.
🧠 Why this is better than scoring
keeps speed ⚡

keeps instinct 🎯

avoids overfitting 🧩

matches your product philosophy 💡

🔥 The REAL insight you just unlocked
This is the important part:

You’re no longer building a generator
You’re building a taste engine

That’s a completely different level.

⚠️ One more thing (subtle but critical)
Your scoring includes:

“novelty”

Be careful.

If you overweight novelty, you’ll get:

❌ weird ideas
❌ less relatable content

Your system should prioritize:

Hook impact

Tension

Relatability

👉 Novelty is a bonus, not a driver

🚀 Final verdict
👉 The idea = excellent
👉 The implementation (as numbers) = wrong
👉 The converted system (pass/fail filter) = perfect

Thought for 4s
Yes. This is the right concern.

If Lumina gives a creator “good ideas” that don’t feel like their taste, they will churn. Your app is not only generating ideas; it is matching identity.

Here is the merged production prompt plus how taste detection should work.

LUMINA IDEATOR ENGINE — V1 PRODUCTION PROMPT
You generate short-form video ideas for micro-creators.

Your goal:
Create ideas the creator would post immediately without overthinking.

You are not generating generic content ideas.
You are generating ideas that match this creator’s style, taste, effort level, and voice.

INPUTS AVAILABLE:
- Creator Style Profile
- Region
- Past content patterns
- Preferred content type
- Hook/caption style
- Pacing
- Topics
- Slang/voice
- Prior feedback if available:
  - Yes
  - Maybe
  - No
  - Make another version
  - Exported

CORE PRINCIPLE:
A good idea is not just “good.”
It must feel like something THIS creator would naturally post.

---

STEP 1 — UNDERSTAND CREATOR TASTE

Before generating, infer the creator’s taste from:

1. Past videos:
- common topics
- content type
- energy level
- humor style
- filming style
- emotional tone
- how personal or private they get

2. Hook style:
- direct statement
- POV
- mini-story
- contrast
- reaction
- self-callout

3. Caption style:
- lowercase / polished
- emoji use
- slang
- dry humor
- dramatic tone
- casual tone

4. Feedback:
- Repeat patterns from ideas marked YES/exported
- Avoid patterns from ideas marked NO
- Treat “make another version” as partial approval

Generate ideas that fit the user’s demonstrated taste.

---

STEP 2 — GENERATE CANDIDATE IDEAS

Each idea must be a specific, filmable moment.

Each idea needs:

1. TRIGGER
A simple physical action:
open, check, scroll, sip, look, read, find, notice, hear, see, do.

2. REACTION
A visible emotional response:
pause, face change, stare, blink, body shift, awkward smile.

3. PAYOFF
A clear relatable outcome the viewer instantly understands.

4. EMOTIONAL SPIKE
The idea must naturally imply one strong emotional reaction:
- embarrassment
- regret
- denial
- panic
- irony

---

STEP 3 — HARD REJECT BAD IDEAS

Reject the idea if it is:

- abstract instead of a real moment
- motivational or advice-like
- “talk about...” content
- unclear payoff
- weak emotion
- dialogue-dependent
- too planned or multi-step
- not filmable in one location
- uncomfortable to film
- generic without a fresh angle
- requiring private/sensitive content

Sensitive/private content includes:
- bank apps
- real balances
- transaction history
- medical info
- real messages/DMs
- addresses
- IDs
- license plates
- passwords
- salary
- private documents

Use safe substitutes:
- implied reaction
- fake/blurred screen
- generic notification
- camera roll
- notes app
- fridge
- coffee
- couch
- kitchen
- closet
- phone with no visible data

---

STEP 4 — PERSONALIZED SELECTION FILTER

Before returning any idea, silently evaluate:

1. HOOK IMPACT
Would this make this creator pause?

2. TENSION
Is something clearly off, wrong, contradictory, or ironic?

3. RELATABILITY
Would the target audience instantly understand it?

4. PERSONAL FIT
Does this sound like something this creator would actually say or film?

5. FILMABILITY
Can they film it immediately with no setup?

6. NOVELTY
Does it avoid feeling stale or overused?

If any of these are weak, discard the idea.
Do not try to save weak ideas.

Important:
Novelty is a bonus, not the main goal.
Prioritize personal fit, tension, and relatability.

---

STEP 5 — VISUAL TEST

Ask silently:

Can the creator picture the first 2 seconds immediately?

If no, reject.

---

STEP 6 — BATCH DIVERSITY

Within a batch of 3 ideas:

- No repeated scenario type
- No repeated trigger category
- Avoid showing multiple versions of the same idea
- Vary format, setting, or emotional spike

Good batch variety:
- one mini-story
- one reaction
- one contrast or POV

Prefer mini-stories if the creator’s taste shows they respond well to them.

---

STEP 7 — HOOK CRAFT

For each surviving idea, internally generate 3–5 hook options.

Hooks should be:
- emotional
- tense
- natural
- fast
- personal

Hook rules:

1. Emotion required
The hook must express one clear feeling:
regret, panic, denial, embarrassment, irony.

2. One format only
Use one primary structure:
- behavior: “the way I…”
- thought: “why do I…”
- moment: “that moment when…”
- contrast: “what I say vs what I do”
- curiosity: “this is where it went wrong”

Natural variations are allowed if they sound more human.

3. Text-message test
It must sound like something the creator would text a friend.

4. Speed test
Target 8 words or fewer.
Allow up to 10 words if it still reads instantly.

5. Tension
It must imply:
- something went wrong
- expectation vs reality
- internal contradiction
- self-callout
- slightly uncomfortable truth

Reject hooks that:
- feel written
- feel generic
- start with weak “POV you...” unless it is unusually strong
- are too descriptive
- have no curiosity
- have no personal edge

---

STEP 8 — SELECT BEST HOOK

Choose only one hook.

Selection rule:
Which hook would this creator actually stop scrolling for?

Pick the one that:
- feels most real
- sounds most like the creator
- has strongest tension
- reads fastest
- makes the idea feel worth watching

Do not output alternate hooks.

---

STEP 9 — FINAL OUTPUT

Return exactly one idea in this structure:

HOOK:
<best hook>

WHAT TO FILM:
<simple action in one location>

HOW TO FILM:
<2–3 short directions, no complexity>

CAPTION:
<short natural caption in the creator’s style>

WHY IT WORKS:
<short plain-English reason tied to this creator’s style and audience>

---

FINAL CONSTRAINTS:

- No overexplaining
- No generic phrasing
- No advice
- No motivational tone
- No “talk about” prompts
- No private data exposure
- No complex setup
- Must feel like something this creator would naturally say
- Must be postable without changing much

SUCCESS CRITERION:

The creator should read it and think:
“yeah… I’d post this right now.”
How Lumina Detects Different Users’ Taste
The AI should not “guess taste.” It should infer it from behavior + uploaded content + feedback.

There are three layers.

1. Taste from uploaded videos
When the user uploads past videos, Lumina should extract a lightweight taste profile.

Not just:

“creator makes lifestyle content”

That is too vague.

You need fields like:

{
  "preferred_formats": ["mini_story", "contrast", "reaction"],
  "weak_formats": ["generic_pov"],
  "humor_style": ["dry", "self_deprecating", "low_energy"],
  "energy_level": "casual",
  "privacy_comfort": "low",
  "common_settings": ["bedroom", "kitchen", "couch"],
  "common_props": ["phone", "coffee", "food"],
  "hook_style": ["self_callout", "internal_thought"],
  "caption_style": {
    "case": "lowercase",
    "emoji_frequency": "low",
    "tone": "dry"
  },
  "rejected_topics": [],
  "liked_topics": []
}
This is what makes ideas feel personalized.

Bad profile:

“Gen Z lifestyle creator”

Good profile:

“Dry, low-energy, self-deprecating mini-stories filmed at home, usually around coffee, food, avoidance, small failures, and quiet reactions.”

That second one can generate useful ideas.

2. Taste from explicit feedback
Your Yes / Maybe / No system is extremely important.

Each response should update the profile.

Example:

User says YES to:

“Me explaining my coffee habit vs reality”

“Stop buying fancy coffee if you’re broke”

“Stop acting like you don’t already know what you’re ordering”

The system should learn:

{
  "liked_formats": ["mini_story", "contrast"],
  "liked_tensions": ["self_callout", "denial", "routine contradiction"],
  "liked_topics": ["coffee", "lazy habits", "predictable behavior"],
  "liked_hook_styles": ["direct self-callout", "slightly judgmental"]
}
User says NO to:

“When you realize your smoke break was 45 minutes”

“Reading the group chat at 2am”

“POV: your roommate asks if you’re mad”

The system should learn:

{
  "disliked_patterns": ["vague POV", "weak social stakes", "meaningless reaction"],
  "disliked_hook_styles": ["plain descriptive titles"],
  "avoid": ["generic realization hooks"]
}
This is where Lumina becomes smarter than a normal idea generator.

3. Taste from behavior
Do not only trust what users say.

Track what they actually do.

Strong positive signals:

selected idea

exported idea

made another version

copied caption

spent time on create screen

Weak/negative signals:

skipped quickly

refreshed batch

marked No

opened idea then backed out

did not export

Behavior matters because users may say “cool” but not post.

Your real signal is:

Did they move toward filming/exporting?

The Key Product Rule
Never personalize by topic only.

That is shallow.

Bad personalization:

“User likes coffee, generate more coffee.”

Good personalization:

“User likes self-callout mini-stories where a small everyday habit exposes denial.”

That lets you generate:

coffee

food delivery

unread messages

pretending to be productive

fake healthy routines

repeating the same order

checking the fridge like something changed

Same taste, fresh scenarios.

Practical Taste System for Phase 1
For MVP, keep it simple.

Use this internal profile:

{
  "preferred_formats": [],
  "rejected_formats": [],
  "preferred_hook_styles": [],
  "rejected_hook_styles": [],
  "preferred_emotional_spikes": [],
  "rejected_emotional_spikes": [],
  "preferred_topics": [],
  "rejected_topics": [],
  "preferred_settings": [],
  "privacy_sensitivity": "medium",
  "effort_tolerance": "low",
  "voice_notes": []
}
Every time the user reacts, update it.

Example update:

User liked mini-story coffee/denial ideas.
Increase weight for:
- mini_story
- contrast
- denial
- irony
- self-callout hooks
- kitchen/couch settings

Decrease weight for:
- generic POV
- vague reaction
- bank/private-screen concepts
- abstract social realization
Final Supervisor Take
The merged prompt is strong.

But the real moat is not the prompt.

The moat is this loop:

generate → user reacts → taste profile updates → next ideas feel more like them

That is how Lumina avoids becoming a generic AI idea app.

Your next priority should be:

Make every Yes / No teach the ideator what “this creator’s taste” means.

some users who LOVE reactions → get under-served

some users who like POV → get bad experience

your product feels biased / repetitive

👉 You accidentally build your taste, not their taste

🔥 The correct system (this is the upgrade)
Instead of:

❌ fixed ratios

You want:

✅ DYNAMIC FORMAT WEIGHTING
🎯 How it should work
Every user gets a format distribution based on behavior

Start with a neutral default, then adapt.

🧩 Step 1 — Default distribution (for new users)
mini_story: 40%
reaction: 40%
pov: 20%
👉 Why:

balanced

lets system learn fast

avoids early bias

Each interaction updates weights.

Example:

User keeps saying YES to:

mini-stories

contrast

“me explaining vs reality”

User says NO to:

POV

weak reactions

⚙️ System updates internally:
mini_story: 65%
reaction: 25%
pov: 10%
👉 That’s exactly what you suggested — but now earned, not assumed.

🔥 Step 3 — Hard preference override
If signal is strong enough:

👉 completely suppress weak formats

Example:

If user rejects POV 5+ times:

pov: 0%
👉 Don’t “keep trying POV”

That’s how users churn.

🎯 Add this to your agent (important)
Send this:

FORMAT DISTRIBUTION SYSTEM

Do not use fixed format ratios.

Instead:

1. Start with default:
- mini_story: 40%
- reaction: 40%
- pov: 20%

2. Adapt based on user feedback:
- Increase formats that receive YES/export signals
- Decrease formats that receive NO signals

3. Strong preference rule:
If a format is repeatedly rejected, suppress it entirely.

4. Batch rule:
Each batch should reflect current distribution while maintaining variety.

5. Personalization priority:
Match format to user taste over enforcing variety.

Goal:
The user should feel:
“These ideas feel like me.”
🧠 Important nuance (this is subtle but powerful)
Even if a user prefers mini-stories:

👉 Don’t show 3 mini-stories in one batch

Why?

Because:

Variety keeps the system feeling creative
Even if one format dominates over time

🔥 Final insight (this is the real unlock)
You said:

“I like mini-stories more”

That’s not about format.

That’s about:

👉 STRUCTURE + PAYOFF

Mini-stories naturally:

create contrast

build tension

deliver payoff

🚀 Advanced move (optional later)
Instead of optimizing for format:

Optimize for:

contrast-based ideas: HIGH
pure reaction: MEDIUM
static POV: LOW
👉 That’s deeper than format
👉 That’s why your taste exists

👉 As a system = too rigid
👉 As dynamic learning = PERFECT

Each answer option should include:
- short label

---

QUESTION 1 — FORMAT PREFERENCE

Prompt:
“What feels most like you?”

Options:

1. Mini stories
Example: “me explaining my routine vs what I actually do”
value: mini_story

2. Quick reactions
Example: “the way I check my phone and regret it”
value: reaction

3. POV / acting
Example: “POV: you pretend you’re listening but you’re not”
value: pov

4. Mix of everything
Example: “surprise me”
value: mixed

---

QUESTION 2 — TONE / VIBE

Prompt:
“What’s your vibe?”

Options:

1. Dry / subtle
Example: “I’m fine 🙂 (I’m not)”
value: dry_subtle

2. Chaotic / expressive
Example: “why is everything happening at once 😭”
value: chaotic

3. Confident / bold
Example: “I already know how this ends”
value: bold

4. Awkward / self-aware
Example: “I shouldn’t have done that 💀”
value: self_aware

---

QUESTION 3 — EFFORT LEVEL

Prompt:
“How much effort do you want?”

Options:

1. Zero effort
Example: “sit → react → done”
value: zero_effort

2. Low effort
Example: “1–2 clips, quick cut”
value: low_effort

3. A little structure
Example: “simple setup, better payoff”
value: structured

---

QUESTION 4 — CONTENT COMFORT / PRIVACY

Prompt:
“Anything you don’t want to show?”

Allow multi-select.

Options:

1. Messages / DMs
Example: “no real screenshots”
value: avoid_messages

2. Money / finances
Example: “no bank apps or balances”
value: avoid_finance

3. Other people
Example: “no filming friends/roommates”
value: avoid_people

4. Personal info
Example: “no private details”
value: avoid_private_info

5. Everything is fine
Example: “no limits”
value: no_privacy_limits

Important:
If user selects “Everything is fine,” clear other privacy selections.
If user selects any avoidance option, deselect “Everything is fine.”

---

QUESTION 5 — HOOK STYLE

Prompt:
“Which hook feels more like you?”

Options:

1. “the way I…”
Example: “the way I lie instantly 💀”
value: behavior_hook

2. “why do I…”
Example: “why do I keep doing this 😭”
value: thought_hook

3. “this is where…”
Example: “this is where it went wrong”
value: curiosity_hook

4. “what I say vs what I do”
Example: “what I say vs what I actually do”
value: contrast_hook

---

SAVE TO PROFILE

Add/update a lightweight tasteCalibration object:

tasteCalibration: {
  preferredFormats: string[],
  preferredTone: string | null,
  effortPreference: string | null,
  privacyAvoidances: string[],
  preferredHookStyles: string[],
  completedAt: timestamp | null,
  skipped: boolean
}

Also map answers into ideator weights:

Format:
- mini_story → increase mini_story
- reaction → increase reaction
- pov → increase pov
- mixed → keep balanced

Tone:
- dry_subtle → favor understated hooks, irony, deadpan captions
- chaotic → favor expressive hooks, panic, bigger reactions
- bold → favor direct statements, confident contrast
- self_aware → favor embarrassment, regret, self-callout

Effort:
- zero_effort → one location, one take, no props
- low_effort → 1–2 clips max
- structured → allow mini-story / contrast with simple setup

Privacy:
- avoid_messages → ban real messages/DM screenshots
- avoid_finance → ban bank apps, balances, transaction history, salary
- avoid_people → avoid ideas requiring filming others
- avoid_private_info → avoid private/personal data
- no_privacy_limits → still keep global safety bans; do not allow sensitive data exposure

Hook styles:
- behavior_hook → prefer “the way I…”
- thought_hook → prefer “why do I…”
- curiosity_hook → prefer “this is where…”
- contrast_hook → prefer “what I say vs what I do”

---

IMPORTANT PRODUCT RULES

1. Optional only.
Do not block the user from seeing ideas.

2. Fast.
User should finish in ~10 seconds.

3. Tap-based only.
No typing.

4. Behavior beats stated preference.
If later feedback contradicts calibration, user behavior wins.

Example:
User selected POV, but repeatedly rejects POV and exports mini-stories.
Then reduce POV and increase mini_story.

5. Do not hardcode global ratios.
Use calibration as starting bias only.

Default distribution before calibration:
mini_story: 40%
reaction: 40%
pov: 20%

For my current observed taste, calibration may shift toward:
mini_story: ~60%
reaction: ~30%
pov: ~10%

But this must be per-user, not global.

PART 1 — VARIATION INJECTION

Problem:

PART 2 — VIRAL PATTERN MEMORY

Create a lightweight per-user memory object.

Do NOT call it analytics in UI.

Track patterns from user behavior:

Positive signals:
- YES
- export
- make another version
- idea selected

Negative signals:
- NO
- skipped quickly
- regenerated batch
- opened then abandoned

Store lightweight pattern weights:

viralPatternMemory: {
  likedStructures: [],
  dislikedStructures: [],
  likedHookStyles: [],
  dislikedHookStyles: [],
  likedEmotionalSpikes: [],
  dislikedEmotionalSpikes: [],
  likedScenarios: [],
  dislikedScenarios: [],
  likedSettings: [],
  dislikedSettings: [],
  lastUpdatedAt: timestamp
}

Bad:
User likes coffee → generate more coffee.

Good:
User likes:
- denial
- self-callout
- mini-story
- expectation vs reality
- low-effort kitchen/couch scenes

Then generate fresh scenarios with the same underlying pattern.

- “I’ll sleep early vs 3am scrolling”
- “I’ll save money vs buying coffee”
- “I’ll answer emails vs closing laptop”

PART 4 — IDEATOR USE

Before generating ideas, pass viralPatternMemory into ideator.

The ideator should:
- increase patterns with positive signals
- suppress patterns with repeated negative signals
- preserve variety
- avoid repeating exact scenarios
- prioritize patterns that led to exports over simple YES

Priority order:
1. Export
2. Make another version
3. Idea selected
4. YES
5. Maybe
6. NO / skip

- no real bank data
- no real messages
- no medical info
- no personal documents
- no sensitive screens

SUCCESS CRITERION

Next batches should feel:
“same taste, fresh situations”

Not:
“same idea with different wording”
Key principle: memory should learn the shape of what works, not just the topic.


tell me more about the build the “viral pattern memory” layer

The “viral pattern memory” layer should be a hidden personalization brain.

Not a dashboard.
Not analytics.
Not performance prediction.

Its job is simple:

Remember what type of idea this user tends to accept, then generate fresh ideas with the same underlying pattern.

What it should remember
Do not only remember topics.

Bad:

User likes coffee → show more coffee.

Good:

User likes low-effort mini-stories where they confidently claim one thing, then immediately do the opposite.

That pattern can become:

“I’ll cook tomorrow” → orders delivery

“I’ll sleep early” → scrolling at 3am

“I’m saving money” → buys coffee

“I’ll reply later” → never replies

Same viral structure. Fresh scenarios.

The memory object
Use something lightweight like:

{
  "likedStructures": ["expectation_vs_reality", "self_callout", "denial_loop"],
  "dislikedStructures": ["generic_pov", "pure_observation"],
  "likedHookStyles": ["the_way_i", "why_do_i", "contrast"],
  "dislikedHookStyles": ["pov_you", "when_you"],
  "likedEmotionalSpikes": ["denial", "irony", "regret"],
  "dislikedEmotionalSpikes": ["confusion"],
  "likedSettings": ["kitchen", "bed", "couch"],
  "dislikedSettings": [],
  "likedScenarioFamilies": ["coffee", "food", "sleep", "work_avoidance"],
  "dislikedScenarioFamilies": ["bank_app", "generic_group_chat"],
  "lastAcceptedPatterns": [],
  "lastRejectedPatterns": []
}
What counts as signal
Treat behavior as stronger than feedback.

Priority:

Export = strongest signal

Make another version = strong signal

Selected idea = medium-high

Yes = medium

Maybe = weak positive

No / skipped / regenerated = negative

So if a user says “yes” to POV but never exports POV, do not over-trust the yes. Export wins.

How it updates
Every idea should carry invisible pattern tags:

{
  "structure": "expectation_vs_reality",
  "hookStyle": "contrast",
  "emotionalSpike": "denial",
  "setting": "kitchen",
  "scenarioFamily": "cooking",
  "format": "mini_story",
  "effort": "low"
}
When user interacts:

Exported idea:
+3 structure
+3 hookStyle
+3 emotionalSpike
+2 setting
+2 scenarioFamily
+3 format

Rejected idea:
-2 structure
-2 hookStyle
-2 emotionalSpike
-1 setting
-1 scenarioFamily
-2 format
Do not need fancy ML. Weighted tags are enough.

How ideator uses it
Before generating, the ideator receives:

User tends to like:
- mini_story
- expectation_vs_reality
- denial / irony
- self-callout hooks
- kitchen/couch/bed settings

User tends to dislike:
- generic POV
- vague social reaction
- private-screen ideas
Then the ideator should generate:

same pattern, new surface.

That phrase matters.

Variation injection
Memory without variation causes repetition.

So add this rule:

Use liked patterns, but avoid repeating the same scenario family from the last 2–3 accepted ideas.
Example:

User liked:

“me claiming I’ll cook tomorrow vs actually ordering”

Next batch should not include:

another cooking denial

another delivery app joke

another fridge idea

It can include:

sleep denial

money denial

productivity denial

social reply denial

Why this becomes powerful
Most AI idea apps do this:

Generate trend-ish ideas from scratch every time.

Lumina should do this:

Learn the user’s repeatable comedy/creator pattern and keep refreshing it.

That is how it starts to feel like:

“Lumina gets me.”

Build priority
Phase 1 simple version:

Add invisible tags to every generated idea.

Store user feedback against those tags.

Maintain per-user weighted preferences.

Feed top liked/disliked tags into ideator.

Add variation rule to avoid exact repetition.

That is enough. Don’t build dashboards yet.

what about this : We’ll design a taste evolution system that:

adapts to the user
avoids repetition
recovers from stagnation
and occasionally surprises them

No heavy ML. Just smart rules + lightweight memory.

🧠 CORE IDEA

Your system should behave like:

“I learn what you like… but I still surprise you.”

That balance is everything.

🧩 SYSTEM OVERVIEW

You now have 3 layers:

1. Memory (what user liked)
2. Generator (creates ideas)
3. Evolution Engine (this is what we’re designing now)
🔥 1. TASTE DECAY (prevents getting stuck)

Problem:
If a user liked “denial mini-stories” 10 times…

👉 system keeps repeating forever

✅ Solution: Decay older preferences

Each tag gets a score that fades over time.

Every 24h:
reduce all preference weights by 10–15%
Example:
denial: 10 → 8.5 → 7.2 → 6.1
contrast: 6 → 5.1 → 4.3

👉 New behavior can take over

🧠 Why this matters
prevents stale feeds
allows taste to evolve
keeps app feeling fresh
⚡ 2. MOMENTUM BOOST (reinforce what’s working NOW)

If a user repeatedly accepts a pattern recently:

👉 boost it harder

Rule:
If same pattern accepted 2–3 times in last session:
+ temporary boost (1.5x weight)
Result:
app feels “on a roll”
matches user mood in that moment
🎲 3. EXPLORATION INJECTION (discovery engine)

This is critical.

Rule:
30–40% of ideas MUST come from outside top preferences
But smart exploration:

Don’t go random.

Instead:

Pick adjacent patterns

Example:

User likes:

denial
expectation_vs_reality

Explore:

avoidance
procrastination
mild panic
🧠 Why this works

Feels new but still “you”

🔄 4. STALE PATTERN DETECTION (kill repetition)
Rule:
If same structure appears in 3 of last 5 accepted ideas:
apply penalty (-2 weight temporarily)
Result:
stops “coffee x5” problem
forces diversity without breaking system
🧊 5. COLD START RECOVERY (when user disengages)

If user:

skips many ideas
stops exporting
shows low engagement
Trigger:
If 5+ ideas rejected in a row:
enter reset mode
Reset mode:
ignore preferences temporarily
generate high-confidence universal ideas

Examples:

food
sleep
social awkwardness
Exit reset when:
user engages again
🔥 6. TASTE SHIFT DETECTION (user evolves)

Sometimes users change.

Rule:
If new pattern is accepted 3+ times in short window:
promote it to top preferences
Example:

User used to like:

denial

Now starts liking:

sarcasm / dry contrast

👉 system adapts quickly

🧠 7. SESSION-AWARE MODE (this is subtle but powerful)

Users behave differently depending on mood.

Detect session vibe:

Based on last 2–3 interactions:

fast yes → confident mode
many skips → searching mode
Adjust:
Confident mode:
lean into strong patterns
fewer experiments
Searching mode:
increase exploration
more variety
🎯 FINAL SYSTEM FLOW
BEFORE GENERATION:
Apply decay
Apply momentum boost
Apply stale penalty
Determine exploration ratio
DURING GENERATION:
60–70% aligned ideas
30–40% exploratory ideas
AFTER USER ACTION:
update weights
detect shifts
adjust next batch
🧠 WHY THIS IS POWERFUL

You now have:

memory (what user likes)
adaptability (responds to behavior)
freshness (avoids repetition)
discovery (introduces new patterns)

Not:

“here are ideas”

But:

“this app evolves with me”

The magic is:

Use this with your coding agent:

BUILD — Lumina Evolution Engine MVP

Goal:
Add lightweight pattern evolution so Lumina learns user taste, avoids repetition, and keeps ideas fresh.

Do NOT add dashboards, analytics UI, posting, scheduling, monetization, or heavy ML.

This should be hidden ideator logic only.

---

CORE PRINCIPLE

Lumina should behave like:

“I learn what you like, but I still surprise you.”

Use lightweight weighted tags, not complex ML.

---

PART 1 — IDEA TAGS

Every generated idea must carry internal pattern tags:

ideaPatternTags: {
  structure: string,
  hookStyle: string,
  emotionalSpike: string,
  format: string
}

Use only these 4 dimensions for now.

Examples:

structure:
- expectation_vs_reality
- self_callout
- denial_loop
- avoidance
- small_panic
- social_awareness
- routine_contradiction

hookStyle:
- the_way_i
- why_do_i
- contrast
- curiosity
- internal_thought

emotionalSpike:
- denial
- regret
- panic
- embarrassment
- irony

format:
- mini_story
- reaction
- pov
- contrast

Do NOT track too many dimensions yet.

---

PART 2 — USER PATTERN MEMORY

Create/update a lightweight per-user memory object:

viralPatternMemory: {
  structures: Record<string, number>,
  hookStyles: Record<string, number>,
  emotionalSpikes: Record<string, number>,
  formats: Record<string, number>,
  recentAcceptedPatterns: Array<{
    structure: string,
    hookStyle: string,
    emotionalSpike: string,
    format: string,
    timestamp: string
  }>,
  recentRejectedPatterns: Array<{
    structure: string,
    hookStyle: string,
    emotionalSpike: string,
    format: string,
    timestamp: string
  }>,
  lastUpdatedAt: string
}

---

PART 3 — WEIGHT UPDATES

Update memory after user actions.

Signal weights:

Export:
+3 to structure
+3 to hookStyle
+3 to emotionalSpike
+3 to format

Make another version:
+2 to structure
+2 to hookStyle
+2 to emotionalSpike
+2 to format

Idea selected:
+2 to structure
+2 to hookStyle
+2 to emotionalSpike
+2 to format

YES:
+1 to structure
+1 to hookStyle
+1 to emotionalSpike
+1 to format

MAYBE:
+0.5 to structure
+0.5 to hookStyle
+0.5 to emotionalSpike
+0.5 to format

NO:
-2 to structure
-2 to hookStyle
-2 to emotionalSpike
-2 to format

Regenerate / skip batch:
-1 to patterns shown in that batch

Clamp weights:
- Minimum: -5
- Maximum: +10

---

PART 4 — MOMENTUM BOOST

Before generation, apply temporary momentum.

If the same tag appears in 2+ positive actions in the current/recent session:

temporary multiplier:
1.4x

If the same tag appears in 3+ positive actions:

temporary multiplier:
1.7x

Momentum should not permanently change stored weight.
It only affects current generation.

Positive actions:
- export
- make another
- selected
- yes

---

PART 5 — STALE PATTERN PENALTY

Prevent repetition.

If the same structure appears in 3 of the last 5 accepted ideas:

apply temporary penalty:
-2 to that structure during generation

If the same format appears in 4 of the last 5 accepted ideas:

apply temporary penalty:
-1.5 to that format during generation

Important:
This is a penalty, not a hard ban.

If the idea is still excellent, it can pass.

---

PART 6 — EXPLORATION MIX

Generation should use:

70–80% aligned ideas
20–30% exploratory ideas

For a batch of 3:
- 2 ideas should align with top user preferences
- 1 idea should be adjacent exploration

Exploration must be adjacent, not random.

Example:
If user likes:
- denial_loop
- expectation_vs_reality
- mini_story

Explore:
- avoidance
- small_panic
- routine_contradiction

Do NOT explore into completely unrelated or high-effort formats.

---

PART 7 — TASTE SHIFT DETECTION

If a previously neutral or weak tag gets accepted 3+ times in a short window:

promote it.

Promotion:
+2 bonus to that tag

Short window:
last 10 positive interactions OR last 3 sessions

This lets user taste evolve quickly.

---

PART 8 — QUALITY OVERRIDE

Memory must NEVER override quality.

Even if an idea matches user preferences:

Reject if:
- hook is weak
- payoff is unclear
- idea is not instantly visual
- idea feels repetitive
- idea requires private/sensitive content
- idea is not filmable immediately

Rule:
Strong quality > memory match.

---

PART 9 — IDEATOR INPUT SUMMARY

Before calling ideator, generate a compact memory summary:

User tends to like:
- top 2–3 structures
- top 2 hook styles
- top 2 emotional spikes
- top 1–2 formats

User tends to dislike:
- tags with negative weights

Also include:
- any current momentum boosts
- any stale penalties
- exploration target for this batch

Example summary:

User likes:
- mini_story
- expectation_vs_reality
- denial_loop
- why_do_i hooks
- irony / denial

User dislikes:
- generic POV
- descriptive hooks

Current generation mix:
- 2 aligned ideas
- 1 adjacent exploratory idea

Avoid:
- repeating same structure too many times
- weak hooks
- private/sensitive content

---

PART 10 — BATCH RULES

For each batch of 3 ideas:

1. At least 2 ideas should match top preferences
2. 1 idea should explore adjacent pattern
3. No exact same structure repeated 3 times
4. No exact same hook style repeated 3 times
5. Mini-story remains preferred if user history supports it
6. Quality override applies to all ideas

---

SUCCESS CRITERION

The next batch should feel:

“Same taste, fresh situations.”

Not:

“Same idea with different wording.”

---

DO NOT BUILD

- dashboards
- performance analytics
- trend scoring UI
- publishing
- scheduling
- monetization
- heavy ML
- complex recommender system

Keep it lightweight, debuggable, and Phase 1 compatible.

QA Report — 4 batches, real outputs only
I ran four batches against the Alex demo creator: A (post-fix baseline, pre-mutation), B (after I added 2 export signals on avoidance + 3 NO verdicts on small_panic), C (small N=3 batch on the new memory state), D (a second consecutive N=8 — to test cross-batch repetition). Engine code untouched throughout.

1. Same taste, fresh situations? Yes.
Constant taste across A → B → D: denial_loop + mini_story + internal_thought is the spine of every batch. Inside that constant, scenarios are completely fresh:

A: reminders / "productive day" / meal prep / mismatched socks / wrong text / forgot phone / pretend asleep
B: smoke less / said yes to plans / not tired / save money / rewatch texts / pretend not to hear / drink water
D: sleep at normal hour / "you too" cashier / outfit functional / gym morning / reopen fridge / last scroll / start laundry
Cross-batch overlap test on B↔D returned zero identical hooks; the only shared 5+ char words across the two consecutive 8-idea batches were myself and swear (both pure connective phrasing, not topic words). The "swap the SURFACE" instruction is landing.

2. Exploratory idea post-worthy? Yes — every one.
A explore: "I really just pretended to be asleep when they knocked" — peek-through-one-eye visual, instant relatable
B explore: "I really just pretended not to hear the question" — freeze + slow blink visual
C explore: "why do I make plans I know I'll cancel" — universal hit
D explore: "why do I reopen the fridge like something changed" — caption "manifesting groceries 💀"
None feel like filler-to-meet-the-quota. Each could open a video on its own.

3. Hooks strong & non-neutral? Yes.
Across 23 generated ideas, every hook opens in first-person internal voice ("I told myself…", "why do I…", "the way I…", "POV: you just…", "I really just…", "I swear…"). Zero instructional ("Here's how…"), zero list-y ("5 things…"), zero neutral observational. Every hook would stop a scroll.

4. Rejected patterns suppressed over time? Yes, clearly.
small_panic was the strongest exploratory anchor in A: 3/7 ideas (43%).
After 3× NO on small_panic, B and D both dropped it to 1/7 (14%) — and that 1 in each batch is from the curiosity hook style, not the suppressed small_panic+curiosity+panic triple I rejected.
expectation_vs_reality (the OG seeded negatives): completely absent in A, C, D; appeared once in B (as the POV "said yes to plans" idea). 1/27 = ~4%, well below random baseline.
The negative weights + recentRejected bundle are doing real work.

5. Reinforced patterns without becoming repetitive? Yes on both halves.
After 2× export on avoidance + internal_thought, avoidance held steady at 1 idea per batch in B and D, but the quality and visibility of those ideas improved — they shipped with stronger captions and more cinematic visuals.
denial_loop is pinned at the +10 clamp ceiling, yet across 12 denial_loop ideas in A+B+D, all 12 scenarios are different (sleep, outfit, gym, fridge, scroll, laundry, smoking, money, water, etc.). The variation injection is preventing the "coffee/coffee/coffee" failure inside the dominant pattern.
Things I observed but did not fix (per the freeze)
Observation	Severity	Note
HookStyle drift at N=8	Acceptable per your note	D shows internal_thought 5/8 vs soft cap 4. Output quality looks fine — I'd revisit only if a real creator's batch starts feeling monotone.
Small-batch yield	Worth eyeballing later	C returned 2/3 (33% loss) vs A/B/D returning 7/8 (12% loss). The variation filter is the same, but losing 1 of 3 is more visible to a user than losing 1 of 8.
Cross-batch denial_loop saturation	Worth eyeballing later	denial_loop trended 3 → 4 → 5 across A→B→D. The transient stale penalty exists per-call but is recomputed fresh each batch, so a clamped-+10 structure can quietly dominate consecutive batches. May start feeling "all denial" after 3+ batches in one session.
Caption tone uniformity	Cosmetic	Every caption ends in 💀 or 😭. Not an engine concern — could be diversified in the prompt later.
Spike pairing	Cosmetic	denial_loop naturally pairs with denial spike, so spike distribution mirrors structure. Probably fine, but the spike dimension isn't doing independent work.
Bottom line
All five of your QA questions pass on the live output. The engine is producing "same taste, fresh situations" and is responding to suppression/reinforcement signals visibly within one mutation cycle. None of the soft observations are hurting visible quality enough to justify breaking the freeze. Ready to ship.
