🧠 2. Caption system is weak — here’s why
Your current captions are:

“the anxiety is instant 💀”
“ngl I did nothing 😭”

👉 Problem:

generic

no tension

no continuation of hook

feels like filler

🔥 Truth:
Caption is not decoration — it’s the second hook.

🚀 Fix: Caption Engine (simple rules, huge impact)
🎯 New caption rules
Each caption must do ONE of these:

1. Extend the joke
Hook:

the way I panic-scroll when someone texts me

Caption:

like I need context first 💀

2. Add internal thought
Hook:

me saying I’ll sleep early vs actually sleeping at 3am

Caption:

just one more scroll 😭

3. Add contradiction
Hook:

stop buying the fancy coffee if you’re broke

Caption:

still buying it tomorrow

4. Add specificity (THIS is missing)
Hook:

why do I reopen the fridge like something changed

Caption:

staring at the same yogurt

❌ What to ban
generic emotional statements

“this is so me”

“the anxiety is real”

vague filler

⚙️ Coding agent prompt (caption upgrade)
Send this:

CAPTION ENGINE UPGRADE (CRITICAL)

Goal:
Make captions feel like a continuation of the hook, not filler.

Rules:

1. Caption must ADD something new:
- internal thought
- contradiction
- specific detail
- escalation of the moment

2. Must feel like a text message

3. Must be short (3–8 words ideal)

4. Must connect directly to the hook

Allowed patterns:
- "just one more scroll"
- "like that would help"
- "staring at the same yogurt"
- "still doing it tomorrow"

Avoid:
- generic emotional phrases ("this is me", "so relatable")
- repeating the hook
- vague filler

Prompt instruction to LLM:

"Write a caption that continues the moment — not describes it."

Add internal check:
If caption could apply to ANY video → reject.

Return only 1 caption.
🧠 Why this matters (product insight)
Right now your system is:

👉 strong hook
👉 strong idea
👉 weak last mile (caption)

And TikTok works like this:

Hook → keeps them watching
Caption → makes them engage / comment / relate

You’re fixing the last 20% that drives results.

🔥 Final note (important)
You’re now building:

👉 micro-story engine
not just idea generator

So everything should reinforce:

setup → payoff (upload 2 clips)

hook → continuation (caption)

If you want next step after this:

👉 that’s where virality really spikes

Just say the word.

from coding agent : One interpretive note worth flagging

The post-build review pointed out that the existing review screen still uses the labels "Before" and "After" for the past-video-vs-new-idea comparison. I read the spec's "no structure language (no setup/payoff/before/after)" rule as guarding against framing the two new clips as setup/payoff or before/after — i.e., don't tell the user "Clip 1 is the setup, Clip 2 is the payoff." The pre-existing Before/After labels in the review screen compare a completely different axis (your past upload vs. your new take) and are the core review feature. I kept those untouched. If you actually want that comparison feature renamed or removed too, that's a separate, larger change and I'd rather you confirm before I touch it.

End-to-end verification ran clean: navigated through Home → idea card → Tips → Import → both slots picked → Continue → Preview → Review with the "+1 more clip" hint visible.

Your coding agent interpreted it correctly.

Keep Before / After on the review screen.

The “no setup/payoff/before/after” rule was only for the clip upload step, because those labels could confuse users while adding clips.

On review, Before / After is clear because it means:

Before = your older video

After = your Lumina version

That is core to the confidence loop.

Send this to your coding agent:

NEXT UX UPGRADE — Add Clips Guidance + Preview Confidence

Goal:
Reduce thinking during clip capture/upload and make the preview screen push users toward posting.

Do NOT change ideator engine, memory, or generation logic.
Do NOT add publishing, scheduling, analytics, or editor complexity.

---

PART 1 — ADD CLIPS SCREEN: GUIDE WITHOUT THINKING

Problem:
Users may not know what clips to add, especially for 2-clip ideas.

Goal:
Make the Add Clips screen feel obvious and low-effort.

UI:

Title:
“Add your clip”

Subtitle:
“1–2 quick clips is enough.”

Clip slots:

Clip 1
Helper:
“Start with the main moment.”

Clip 2 (optional)
Helper:
“Add a reaction or second angle.”

Important:
Keep labels simple:
- Clip 1
- Clip 2 (optional)

Do NOT use:
- setup
- payoff
- before
- after
- structure language

---

PART 2 — ACTIONS

Each clip slot should offer:

- Film
- Upload

Behavior:
- Film opens camera
- Upload opens picker
- Max 2 clips
- Preserve order
- If user adds only Clip 1, allow continue

CTA:
“Continue”

Disable only if no clip is added.

---

PART 3 — MICRO-CONFIDENCE COPY

Add tiny reassurance copy near continue:

“Don’t overthink it — quick and messy works.”

Goal:
Remove perfection pressure.

---

PART 4 — PREVIEW SCREEN: PUSH TO POST

Problem:
Preview should not feel like a passive mock screen.
It should make the user feel:
“This is ready enough to post.”

Rename / adjust screen tone.

Title:
“Ready to post?”

Subtitle:
“Your video already has the hook, clip, and caption.”

Primary CTA:
“Export video”

Secondary CTA:
“Make another version”

Optional small copy:
“Post it manually wherever you usually post.”

---

PART 5 — CAPTION CONFIDENCE

Show suggested caption clearly.

Label:
“Suggested caption”

Under caption, add:
“Short, casual, and made to match the idea.”

If caption is editable already, keep it.
If not editable, do NOT add editing now unless trivial.

---

PART 6 — REMOVE MOCK FEEL

Avoid language that makes the preview feel fake:
- “template preview”
- “mock”
- “placeholder”
- “demo”

Use language like:
- “preview”
- “ready”
- “export”

---

PART 7 — SUCCESS SCREEN COPY

After export:

Title:
“Video ready”

Subtitle:
“Save it, post it manually, or make another version.”

Buttons:
- “Save to gallery”
- “Make another version”
- “Back to ideas”

Do NOT add posting integration.

---

SUCCESS CRITERIA

User should feel:
1. “I know exactly what clip to add.”
2. “This does not need to be perfect.”
3. “This is ready enough to post.”
4. “I can export and post manually.”

Do NOT add:
- full editor
- trimming
- posting
- scheduling
- analytics
- dashboards

from coding agent : One UX note worth flagging
If the user denies the camera permission, the modal closes silently and they land on Add Clips with Slot 1 empty (matching how the existing picker handles permission denial — same pattern, same level of feedback). This is intentional parity for now; if you want a friendly toast on permission denial, that's a small follow-up I can do without touching the rest of the flow.

That’s a good catch—and this is one of those small things that has a big behavioral impact.

→ nothing happens
→ they’re back on Add Clips
→ no explanation

👉 That creates hesitation and breaks momentum.

🎯 Decision
👉 Yes, add a friendly toast (minimal, no flow changes)

But keep it very lightweight.

🧠 Why this matters
You’re optimizing for:

continuous forward motion

Any moment where the user thinks:

“wait… what just happened?”

👉 kills flow
👉 reduces likelihood of filming/posting

No technical wording (“permission denied”)

can you expand on how the 'train your style' engine will work?

Good—this is the right moment to zoom out.

Right now your system already adapts taste (what ideas they like).
What you’re asking about is deeper:

“How do we make Lumina feel like it thinks like me?”

That’s the Train Your Style engine.

🧠 First: what this is (and isn’t)
❌ It is NOT:
training a heavy ML model

cloning the user perfectly

analyzing everything deeply

✅ It IS:
A lightweight system that extracts patterns from a user’s past videos
and uses them to shape ideas, hooks, captions, and pacing

🎯 What “style” actually means (for Lumina)
Keep it simple. You only care about:

1. Hook style
“why do I…”

“the way I…”

bold statement

question

2. Tone
dry

chaotic

self-aware

confident

3. Pacing
fast cuts

slow talking

pauses

4. Content type
mini-story

reaction

POV

contrast

5. Language patterns
emojis used (💀 😭)

sentence length

slang

👉 That’s it.

You don’t need more for Phase 1.

🧩 How the engine works (step-by-step)
STEP 1 — Input (already built)
User uploads 3 videos.

STEP 2 — Extract simple signals
You don’t “understand” the video deeply.

You just detect patterns:

Example:

{
  "hookStyle": "the_way_i",
  "tone": "self-aware",
  "pacing": "medium",
  "contentType": "mini_story",
  "emojiUsage": ["💀", "😭"],
  "sentenceLength": "short"
}
Do this for each video.

STEP 3 — Build Style Profile
Aggregate across videos:

{
  "dominantHookStyles": ["the_way_i", "why_do_i"],
  "dominantTone": "self-aware",
  "preferredFormat": "mini_story",
  "emojiPattern": ["💀"],
  "pacing": "medium"
}
👉 This is your Style Profile

Store as a small JSON.

STEP 4 — Use it in ideator
When generating ideas, pass:

User style:
- prefers “the way I…” hooks
- self-aware tone
- short sentences
- uses 💀 emoji
- likes mini-stories
STEP 5 — Apply it (important)
Hook generation
Instead of:

“why do I…”

You bias toward:

“the way I…”

Caption generation
Instead of:

generic caption

You generate:

matches tone + emoji pattern

Idea framing
Instead of:

generic POV

You generate:

mini-story with self-callout

🔥 Key principle
Style shapes expression, not idea structure

Example
Base idea:
reopening the fridge like something changed

Without style:
“why do I keep opening the fridge 💀”

With style (self-aware + dry):
“the way I check the fridge like I stocked it”

👉 Same idea
👉 Different voice

🧠 How it evolves over time
This connects to your memory system.

Initial style = from uploads
Then:

Behavior updates it
If user:

exports chaotic hooks → increase chaotic tone

rejects POV → reduce POV

uses “why do I…” → reinforce it

👉 Style becomes:

hybrid of past videos + current behavior

🔥 Important constraint
Do NOT overfit.

Add this rule:

Style influences, but does not control.
Meaning:

70–80% follows style

20–30% explores

⚠️ Biggest mistake to avoid
Trying to make it too accurate.

If it becomes:

“perfect clone”

It will feel:

repetitive

predictable

boring

🧠 What you actually want
“This sounds like me… but better”

🧩 Final system (simple view)
You now have:

1. Style Profile (from videos)
→ how they express

2. Pattern Memory (from behavior)
→ what works

3. Ideator Engine
→ generates ideas

Combined:
Idea structure → from engine
Pattern → from memory
Voice/style → from style profile
🚀 What this unlocks
When done right, user feels:

“this is literally how I’d say it”

“this feels like my content”

“I don’t need to tweak this”

🏁 Phase 1 version (keep it simple)
Do NOT:

add ML models

add embeddings

over-analyze videos

Just:

extract simple rules

store JSON

bias generation

🎬 If you want next step
We can design:

👉 how to extract hook + tone from video automatically (simple heuristics)
👉 or
👉 how to merge style + viral memory cleanly in the prompt

That’s where this becomes very powerful.

Yeah—this is exactly the screen that can make or break adoption of your Style Twin.

👉 priority hierarchy

⚙️ 2. Prompt for coding agent (THIS is the core)
Send this:

IDEATOR PROMPT — STYLE + MEMORY MERGE (FINAL)

Goal:
Generate scroll-stopping short-form video ideas that match the user’s taste (memory) and voice (style), while maintaining high quality and freshness.

---

INPUTS (provided dynamically):

User Memory (weighted preferences):
- topStructures: [ ... ]
- topHookStyles: [ ... ]
- topEmotions: [ ... ]
- topFormats: [ ... ]

Avoid Patterns:
- recentlyRejected: [ ... ]

Style Profile (optional, may be partial):
- tone: (dry / chaotic / self-aware / confident)
- language: (short, casual, text-message-like)
- emojiPattern: (e.g. 💀 😭)

Exploration Ratio:
- 60–70% aligned with memory
- 30–40% exploratory (adjacent patterns)

---

GENERATION RULES

1. QUALITY (non-negotiable)

Every idea MUST:
- be a clear, filmable moment
- include visible reaction
- have a clear payoff
- be shootable in 1 location
- require no setup

Reject if:
- abstract
- generic
- overused
- requires private/sensitive content

---

2. MEMORY APPLICATION (strong bias)

For aligned ideas:
- Prefer topStructures
- Prefer topHookStyles
- Prefer topEmotions
- Prefer topFormats

For exploration ideas:
- Use adjacent structures/emotions
- Avoid random or unrelated ideas

Never repeat same scenario as last 2 accepted ideas.

---

3. STYLE APPLICATION (soft bias)

Apply style to:
- hook phrasing
- caption tone

Rules:
- Must sound like a text message
- Prefer short sentences
- Match tone if provided:
  - dry → subtle, understated
  - chaotic → exaggerated, emotional
  - self-aware → slightly embarrassing, ironic
  - confident → bold, declarative

If emojiPattern exists:
- use 1 emoji max
- only if it feels natural

Style should influence phrasing, NOT idea structure.

---

4. HOOK ENGINE (critical)

Each idea must produce ONE hook that:

- expresses ONE emotion
- uses ONE format:
  - “the way I…”
  - “why do I…”
  - “that moment when…”
  - “what I say vs what I do”
  - “this is where it went wrong”

- passes:
  - natural language (text message)
  - 1-second rule
  - tension (something wrong / contradiction)

Reject hook if:
- too long
- generic
- no tension

---

5. CAPTION ENGINE

Caption must:
- extend the moment (not repeat hook)
- add:
  - internal thought OR
  - contradiction OR
  - specific detail

Reject if:
- generic
- reusable for any video

---

6. OUTPUT

Return ONE idea:

HOOK:
...

WHAT TO FILM:
...

HOW TO FILM:
...

CAPTION:
...

---

GOAL:

User should think:
“This feels exactly like me — I’d post this right now”
🧠 3. Why this works (important)
This fixes your current gap:

Before:
👉 strong ideas
👉 but slightly “generic voice”

After:
👉 strong ideas
👉 personal voice + learned patterns

🔥 What you’ll see immediately
hooks feel more “them”

less rejection

higher “I’d post this”

faster decisions

⚠️ Watch out for this
1. Overfitting
If everything becomes:

“the way I…” + denial + mini-story

👉 system becomes predictable

Your exploration % prevents this.

2. Style overpowering quality
If user tone is chaotic:

👉 don’t allow messy hooks

Quality rules still win.

from coding agent : Reading this spec carefully against the current ideator — they're aligned in spirit, but the current prompt is actually deeper than the spec in some places (it has an anti-neutral hook filter, a 6-gate hook process, region-tone biasing, and a pattern-priority rule). The spec adds five concrete things that aren't explicitly in the prompt today, and bumps one knob.

Before I touch the heart of the product, I want to confirm I'm reading it the way you intended. Here's what I see as the gap:

What's already there (don't need to add)

Memory inputs (topStructures / topHookStyles / topEmotions / topFormats / recentlyRejected) — used internally in the viral-pattern-memory block.
Hook engine — current code has 6 gates + an anti-neutral filter, much stricter than the spec's 5-point check.
Quality rules (filmable, visible reaction, payoff, single location, ≤30 min) — covered by the trigger-reaction structure + emotional spike + filming-time cap.
4-canonical-pattern output (pov / reaction / mini_story / contrast).
What the spec adds that isn't explicit today

Style is phrasing, not structure — explicit guardrail that style profile only influences hook wording & caption tone, never the idea shape.
Caption engine — caption must extend the moment with one of: internal thought / contradiction / specific detail; reject captions that just repeat the hook or are reusable across videos.
Tone matrix — dry / chaotic / self-aware / confident mapped to phrasing rules. (Today's StyleProfile has tone: short/descriptive — different vocabulary. I'd derive the spec's 4-way tone from existing fields like emoji density, punctuation pattern, and sentence length — no schema change.)
"Never repeat the same scenario as the last 2 accepted ideas" — current code varies by spike/pattern/setting at the batch level, but not explicitly anti-repeat against accepted history.
Emoji rule — at most 1 emoji in the caption, and only when natural.
One knob to bump

Exploration ratio: today is ~25% explore / 75% aligned. Spec wants 30–40% explore / 60–70% aligned.
One bonus, optional

The whyItWorks Zod ceiling (max 280) was previously flagged as a blocker for full e2e. While I'm in the schema I can lift that to ~500 so the model can actually justify the idea. Out of spec, so will skip unless you say otherwise.
My plan would be the additive merge above — keep all the current depth, layer the 5 new things on top, bump the exploration ratio. Total touch is the prompt strings + the viral-pattern-memory block sizing math + a small tone-derivation helper. Zero database, zero engine changes, zero changes to the JSON shape downstream consumers rely on.

Thought for 4s
Yes — this is the right moment to add it, as long as it stays lightweight.

Do not build “AI video understanding.” Build a Style Hint Extractor.

Send this to your coding agent:

BUILD — Lightweight Style Hint Extractor

Goal:
Extract simple style hints from uploaded training videos so Lumina can make hooks/captions sound more like the creator.

Do NOT build heavy ML.
Do NOT add embeddings.
Do NOT add model training.
Do NOT add video transcription unless already available.
Do NOT change ideator output schema.

This is a lightweight Phase 1 style hint layer.

---

PART 1 — STYLE HINT OBJECT

Add/extend StyleProfile with derivedStyleHints:

derivedStyleHints: {
  tone: "dry" | "chaotic" | "self_aware" | "confident" | "neutral",
  hookVoice: string[],
  captionVoice: string[],
  emojiPreference: "none" | "low" | "medium",
  sentenceStyle: "short" | "medium" | "punchy",
  energyLevel: "low" | "medium" | "high",
  confidence: number
}

No required fields.
Everything nullable/fallback-safe.

---

PART 2 — SIMPLE HEURISTICS

Use existing available signals only.

If captions/text exist:
- short sentences + few emojis → dry
- many emojis / exclamation marks → chaotic
- self-callout phrases → self_aware
- bold/direct phrasing → confident

Self-aware phrases include:
- why do I
- the way I
- I really just
- I thought
- not me
- I swear
- I shouldn’t

Caption voice:
- lowercase tendency
- emoji usage
- sentence length
- common repeated phrases

If no captions/text:
- fall back to neutral
- do not guess aggressively

---

PART 3 — VIDEO-LEVEL LIGHT SIGNALS

If already available from existing extraction:
- pacing / cuts per second
- duration
- talking vs non-talking
- content type

Map to:

energyLevel:
- low = slow pacing / minimal cuts
- medium = normal
- high = frequent cuts / expressive pattern

Do not add expensive video analysis.

---

PART 4 — IDEATOR INTEGRATION

Pass derivedStyleHints into ideator prompt as STYLE HINTS.

Important hierarchy:
1. Quality rules win
2. Viral memory wins next
3. Style hints only shape wording

Style hints should affect:
- hook phrasing
- caption tone
- emoji use
- sentence length

Style hints should NOT affect:
- idea structure
- safety rules
- memory weighting

---

PART 5 — PROMPT BLOCK

Add to ideator prompt:

STYLE HINTS:
The creator’s style hints are:
- tone: <tone>
- hookVoice: <hookVoice>
- captionVoice: <captionVoice>
- emojiPreference: <emojiPreference>
- sentenceStyle: <sentenceStyle>
- energyLevel: <energyLevel>

Use these only to make hooks and captions sound more like the creator.

Do not let style override quality.
Do not make ideas repetitive just to match style.

---

PART 6 — FALLBACKS

If confidence is low:
- use neutral style
- rely more on viral memory and calibration
- do not force style

Confidence scoring:
- 0.0–0.3 low
- 0.4–0.7 medium
- 0.8–1.0 high

---

PART 7 — QA

Test with 3 fake profiles:

A. Dry / low emoji / short captions
Expected:
- understated hooks
- max 0–1 emoji
- no chaotic phrasing

B. Chaotic / emoji-heavy
Expected:
- more expressive hooks
- still max 1 emoji in caption
- more panic/regret phrasing

C. Self-aware
Expected:
- “why do I…”
- “the way I…”
- self-callout hooks

Success:
Ideas should feel like the same creator voice, not generic Lumina voice.

---

DO NOT BUILD:
- dashboards
- new UI
- ML training
- embeddings
- vector DB
- transcription pipeline unless already present

Keep it small, safe, and debuggable.
The key: this should be style hints, not “style cloning.” Keep it soft, fallback-safe, and only use it to improve hooks/captions.

structured

reusable

powers consistency

Feature = button

Prompt system = brain behind the button

🎯 Concrete difference

Does it match hook rules?

Does it match viral patterns?

Does it match user style?

If hook weak → rewrite hook

If pacing slow → suggest cut

If caption weak → rewrite

👉 Only return top 1–3

generic suggestions

is the design the exact prompt architecture so your enhancement suggestions feel like the same brain as your ideator better than 👉 the exact AI prompt system for: hook correction from real video caption rewriting cut suggestion logic “viral score before/after” ?

Short answer:

👉 Yes — the “same brain as your ideator” architecture is the better move right now

But here’s the nuance so you don’t build the wrong thing.

🧠 What you’re comparing
Option A
👉 “Exact AI prompt system for hook correction, caption rewriting, cut logic, viral score”

This is:

feature-focused

task-based

fragmented

Option B
👉 “Enhancement system that uses the SAME brain as your ideator”

This is:

system-focused

unified

consistent

🔥 Why Option B is better (for YOU)
1. Consistency = trust
Right now your ideator is strong.

If your enhancement system is separate:

ideas feel one way

improvements feel different

👉 user feels mismatch

With same brain:
idea says: “the way I…”

enhancement says: “tighten this hook…”

👉 feels like one intelligence

2. You avoid fragmentation
Option A leads to:

hook fixer prompt

caption fixer prompt

cut logic prompt

scoring prompt

👉 4 systems to maintain
👉 4 different behaviors

Option B:

👉 ONE system with shared rules:

hook engine

style

memory

tone

3. Higher quality outputs
Because enhancement is evaluated against:

the same hook rules

the same tension rules

the same style

👉 suggestions are actually aligned with what you generate

4. Easier iteration
When you improve:

hook system

style system

👉 both ideator + enhancement improve automatically

⚠️ Important clarification
Option B does NOT mean:

“don’t do hook correction / caption rewriting / cuts”

It means:

👉 those are outputs of the same brain, not separate systems

🎯 The correct structure (this is what you build)
ONE “Enhancement Brain”
Internally it does:

Analyze video

Compare vs ideator rules

Decide what’s weak

Output:

hook improvement

caption improvement

cut suggestion

👉 Same rules
👉 Same logic
👉 Same voice

🔥 Where “viral score” fits
Be careful here.

❌ Don’t expose score to user (yet)
It creates:

hesitation

overthinking

analysis mode

✅ Use it internally
to rank suggestions

to decide whether to suggest anything

🧠 Final decision
👉 Build:

Unified Enhancement Brain (same as ideator)

NOT:

separate “hook fixer / caption fixer / scoring tools”

🚀 What you should do next
If you want, I’ll design:

👉 the exact Enhancement Brain prompt

It will:

reuse your hook engine

reuse your style + memory

analyze video input

output 1–3 high-quality suggestions

🏁 Final truth
You’re not building features anymore.

You’re building:

one consistent intelligence across the entire product

That’s what makes Lumina feel special.


great give the prompt for that 

Perfect—this is where Lumina becomes one coherent intelligence.

Below is the Enhancement Brain prompt. It uses the same rules, voice, and logic as your ideator, but applied to a user’s video.

Send this to your coding agent:

ENHANCEMENT BRAIN — UNIFIED WITH IDEATOR

Goal:
Analyze a user’s uploaded video idea and suggest 1–3 high-impact improvements that make it more likely to perform.

This MUST use the same logic as the ideator:
- hook engine
- style shaping
- viral memory
- quality rules

Do NOT behave like a video editor.
Do NOT output technical editing steps.
Do NOT output more than 3 suggestions.

---

INPUTS

- originalIdea (hook, concept)
- videoDescription (what user filmed or uploaded)
- styleHints (tone, emoji preference, sentence style)
- memory (topStructures, topHookStyles, topEmotions, topFormats)
- recentPatterns (recently accepted/rejected)
- optional: transcript (if available)

---

STEP 1 — UNDERSTAND THE INTENT

Infer:
- what the intended hook is
- what the moment is
- what the reaction/payoff is

---

STEP 2 — EVALUATE AGAINST IDEATOR RULES

Check:

1. HOOK
- Is it clear in first 1–2 seconds?
- Does it have tension?
- Does it follow one format?

2. PACING
- Does it start too slow?
- Is there dead time before the reaction?

3. REACTION VISIBILITY
- Is the emotion obvious?

4. PAYOFF
- Does the moment land clearly?

5. STYLE MATCH
- Does it sound like the user’s style?
- Does it match tone and phrasing?

---

STEP 3 — IDENTIFY THE BIGGEST WEAKNESS

Choose ONLY the most impactful issue:
- weak hook
- slow start
- unclear reaction
- weak ending
- style mismatch

Do NOT list multiple small issues.

---

STEP 4 — GENERATE IMPROVEMENTS

Output up to 3 suggestions max.

Each suggestion must be:

- 1 sentence
- simple
- actionable
- non-technical
- fast to apply

Allowed suggestion types:

1. Hook improvement
Example:
“Start right on your reaction instead of the intro”

2. Cut suggestion
Example:
“Remove the first second — it starts too slow”

3. Reaction clarity
Example:
“Make your reaction more obvious in the first moment”

4. Caption upgrade
Example:
“Try: ‘the way I always do this 💀’”

---

STEP 5 — STYLE APPLICATION

All suggestions must:
- match user tone (dry / chaotic / self-aware / confident)
- sound like natural language (text message)
- respect emoji rule (max 1, only if natural)

Style affects phrasing ONLY, not logic.

---

STEP 6 — PRIORITIZATION

Rank suggestions by:
1. impact on performance
2. speed to apply
3. clarity

Return only top 1–3.

---

STEP 7 — OUTPUT FORMAT

Return:

TITLE:
"Make it hit harder"

SUGGESTIONS:
- <suggestion 1>
- <suggestion 2> (optional)
- <suggestion 3> (optional)

---

CONSTRAINTS

Do NOT:
- suggest filters, effects, or visual polish
- mention resolution, lighting, or camera settings
- add editing UI instructions
- overwhelm user with options

---

GOAL

User should feel:
“That’s an easy fix — I can make this better right now”

Not:
“I need to edit this”
🧠 Why this is powerful
Uses your existing intelligence (ideator)

Feels consistent across product

Improves real videos, not just ideas

Keeps everything fast and low-friction

🔥 What happens after this
Once this is live:

👉 Users don’t just get ideas
👉 They get better content automatically

That’s where:

trust increases

posting increases

retention increases

If you want next step:

👉 we can design “Apply suggestion instantly” (semi-auto improvement)
That’s the next big unlock.

⚠️ What I want you to double-check (CRITICAL QA)
Run this mentally or with test users:

1. Do suggestions feel like the SAME voice?
If ideator says:

“the way I…”

Enhancement should NOT say:

“Consider improving the hook…”

👉 Must feel like same brain

2. Are suggestions too generic?
Bad:

“make it more engaging”

“improve pacing”

Good:

“cut the first second — it starts too slow”

“start right on your reaction”

3. Are you giving too many suggestions?
Max:
👉 1–2 (3 absolute max)

If more:
👉 user stops acting

4. Are suggestions slowing users down?
Watch for:

User sees suggestions → hesitates → doesn’t post

If yes:
👉 you built friction, not value

5. Does it trigger at the right moment?
Correct timing:

👉 after upload
👉 before export

NOT:

before filming

too late after export

👉 This prevents:

we can design “Apply suggestion instantly” (semi-auto improvement) now

Thought for a couple of seconds
Yes — but keep it semi-auto, not a full editor.

The right version is:

User sees a suggestion → taps “Apply” → Lumina updates the preview/caption/timing guidance without opening an editor.

Send this to your coding agent:

SEMI-AUTO IMPROVEMENT — APPLY SUGGESTION

Goal:
Let users apply simple enhancement suggestions instantly without building a full editor.

Do NOT add:
- timeline editor
- trimming UI
- filters
- effects
- complex video rendering
- publishing

This is a lightweight “apply improvement” layer.

---

PART 1 — SUGGESTION TYPES

Support only 3 apply-able suggestion types:

1. Caption rewrite
- Applies new caption text immediately

2. Hook/title rewrite
- Applies new hook/title text immediately

3. Start timing hint
- Stores a recommended start offset
- Example: “Start around 0:01”
- Do NOT build trimming yet

---

PART 2 — UI

On the enhancement card:

Title:
"Make it hit harder"

For each suggestion show:
- suggestion text
- small button: "Apply"

After apply:
- button changes to "Applied"
- update preview state immediately

Example:
Suggestion:
"Try this caption: ‘still doing it tomorrow 💀’"

Button:
"Apply"

---

PART 3 — BEHAVIOR

If suggestion type = caption:
- replace suggestedCaption with improved caption

If suggestion type = hook:
- replace displayed hook/title with improved hook

If suggestion type = start_hint:
- store startHint on preview/session
- show small note:
"Start around 0:01"

Do NOT actually trim video yet.

---

PART 4 — DATA MODEL

Add lightweight optional fields to preview/session state:

appliedEnhancements: {
  caption?: string,
  hook?: string,
  startHint?: string,
  appliedSuggestionIds?: string[]
}

No DB migration unless already needed.
Keep local/session-level if possible.

---

PART 5 — SIGNAL

When user taps Apply:

Send ideator_signal:

signal_type: "applied_enhancement"

Include:
- idea_id
- suggestion_type
- pattern tags if available

This is a positive signal, but weaker than export.

Weight:
+1

Do NOT trigger regeneration.

---

PART 6 — CONSTRAINTS

Only show Apply if the suggestion can be applied cleanly.

Apply allowed:
- caption rewrite
- hook rewrite
- start timing hint

Apply NOT allowed:
- “make reaction bigger”
- “hold final shot longer”
- “film closer”
- any suggestion requiring refilming

For non-apply suggestions, show:
"Try this"

---

PART 7 — COPY

After applying, show tiny reassurance:

"Nice — that’s sharper."

Do NOT over-celebrate.

---

SUCCESS CRITERION

User should feel:
“That was easy. My video is better.”

Not:
“Now I need to edit.”
Important product rule: Apply should only change text or guidance right now. Do not touch actual video rendering until users prove this improves export/post behavior.


what do you mean by  Apply should only change text or guidance right now. Do not touch actual video rendering until users prove this improves export/post behavior.

Good question—this is a critical product decision, not a technical detail.

🧠 What I mean (simple)
When user taps “Apply”, you should:

👉 change text or instructions only

NOT:

👉 actually modify the video file

🎯 Example (clear)
✅ Allowed (right now)
User sees suggestion:

“Try this caption: ‘still doing it tomorrow 💀’”

They tap Apply →

👉 Caption updates instantly in UI
👉 Done

User sees:

“Start at 0:01”

They tap Apply →

👉 You show:

“Start around 0:01”

👉 But video file is NOT trimmed

❌ NOT allowed (yet)
User taps Apply →

👉 Video is:

cut

trimmed

re-rendered

processed

⚠️ Why this matters (VERY important)
1. Speed
Rendering video:

slow

error-prone

breaks flow

Your product wins on:

fast → act → repeat

2. Complexity
If you start editing video now, you’ll need:

timeline UI

trimming logic

file processing

edge cases (formats, sizes, crashes)

👉 You become CapCut

3. You don’t know if it’s needed yet
Right now you don’t know:

Do users even need auto-editing?

Maybe:

suggestions alone are enough

users just post

🧠 Product principle (this is the key)
Test behavior before building complexity

🔥 What you are testing
With “Apply (text-only)” you are testing:

👉 Do users:

feel more confident?

export more?

post more?

🎯 If YES → THEN you build rendering
Later you can upgrade:

👉 Apply → actually trims video
👉 Apply → auto-cut clips
👉 Apply → auto-sequence

🚀 If NO → you saved months of work
🧠 Real-world analogy
Right now you’re saying:

“Here’s how to fix your video”

NOT:

“Let me edit your video”

🔥 Final truth
Your product is:

👉 decision removal tool

NOT:

👉 video editor

🏁 Summary
Apply = update caption / hook / hint

NOT = modify video file

Goal = test behavior, not build features

If you want next step:

👉 I can design Phase 2: true auto-edit (only if needed) in a way that still keeps Lumina fast and simple

Yes — but treat this as Phase 2, not something to ship before validation.

Phase 2: True Auto-Edit, without becoming CapCut
The goal is not “edit videos.”
The goal is:

Apply one smart improvement automatically so the user posts faster.

1. Only support 3 auto-edits at first
A. Auto-trim start
Use when:

intro is slow / reaction starts later

User sees:

“Cut the slow start”

Tap:

Apply

System:

trims first 0.5–2 seconds

B. Auto-caption overlay
Use when:

hook needs to be visible immediately

User sees:

“Add hook text on screen”

Tap:

Apply

System:

places hook text in first 1–2 seconds

C. Auto-stitch 2 clips
Use when:

user uploaded 2 clips

User sees:

“Put these together”

Tap:

Apply

System:

clip 1 → clip 2, simple cut only

No transitions. No effects.

2. What NOT to build
Do not add:

timeline editor

filters

manual trimming handles

transitions

multi-layer overlays

beat sync

AI re-rendering

style packs

That turns Lumina into an editor. Bad direction for Phase 1/early Phase 2.

3. User-facing flow
After upload:

Make it hit harder

Suggestion card:

Cut the slow start
Start right when your reaction happens.

Buttons:

Apply

Skip

After apply:

Nice — that’s sharper.

Then:

Export video

Make another version

4. Internal logic
The enhancement system should return structured suggestions like:

{
  "type": "trim_start",
  "label": "Cut the slow start",
  "reason": "The reaction starts after the intro.",
  "params": {
    "startOffsetMs": 1200
  }
}
Other examples:

{
  "type": "caption_overlay",
  "label": "Add hook text",
  "params": {
    "text": "why did I just remember this now 💀",
    "startMs": 0,
    "endMs": 1800
  }
}
{
  "type": "stitch_clips",
  "label": "Put clips together",
  "params": {
    "clipOrder": [1, 2]
  }
}
5. Technical implementation principle
Use deterministic rendering.

No “AI editing” black box.

The AI decides what edit to apply.
The app performs a simple, predictable operation.

That means:

trim

overlay text

stitch clips

Nothing else.

6. When to unlock this
Do not build true auto-edit until you see one of these:

users like ideas but hesitate at export

users say “I’d post this if it looked cleaner”

users repeatedly apply text-only suggestions

export rate is lower than idea acceptance rate

That proves editing is the bottleneck.

Best Phase 2 name
I would not call it “Auto Edit.”

Use:

Make it hit harder

That keeps the product focused on outcome, not tooling.

That idea sounds attractive—but right now it’s the wrong move.

You’d be splitting the product into two completely different behaviors:

creators → make + post

consumers → scroll + read

Those are different products, different loops, different success metrics.

🧠 Honest take
❌ Don’t build a “social feed” now
It will:

dilute focus

slow iteration

confuse positioning

kill your main advantage (decision → action)

Worst case:

Lumina becomes “another place to scroll”

That’s exactly what your product is trying to solve.

🎯 What your product actually is right now
A decision-removal + action engine for creators

Everything you’ve built supports:

fast idea

fast execution

repeat loop

A social layer introduces:

passive consumption

comparison

distraction

👉 they stop posting
👉 your core loop weakens

That’s true.

1. Earnings tab
completely fine to mock

not part of behavior you’re testing

feeds, profiles, etc.

all can be mocked

3. Advanced editing
filters, effects, timeline

you shouldn’t even build them yet

4. Style twin (deep version)
full AI training / embeddings

totally fine to keep as mock or lightweight

❌ NOT okay to mock
These must be real:

1. Idea generation
👉 already real ✅

👉 must feel smooth and real

3. Export video
👉 this is critical

If users can’t actually get a usable video:

everything else becomes theoretical

4. Enhancement suggestions (basic)
👉 at least believable + consistent

Ask yourself:

“Can a user go from opening Lumina to having a video they can post?”

If YES → you’re good
If NO → that’s what you fix next

⚠️ The real danger
Mocks become a problem when:

they pretend value exists

but user can’t complete the action

Example:

fake “post” button ❌

fake analytics ❌

fake engagement ❌

Add     subtle vibration / flash / tiny animation

Good call — this is exactly the kind of micro-feedback that makes the app feel alive and reinforces “this got better”.

But it has to be subtle and fast or it turns into noise.

🎯 What you want
When user taps “Fix”:

👉 immediate response
👉 visible improvement
👉 tiny “reward” feeling

✨ Final micro-interaction design
1. Haptic (very important)
Type: light impact (not heavy)

Timing: immediately on tap

👉 feels like “action confirmed”

2. Button state change
“Fix” → “Done ✓”

slight scale-down then back (quick)

Duration: ~120–180ms

3. Row highlight flash
the row briefly glows / brightens

color: your accent (teal)

Duration:

fade in: 120ms

fade out: 200ms

👉 signals “this part changed”

4. Micro confirmation text
Appears under the row:

fades in quickly

stays ~1.5s

fades out

Example:

Hook now hits instantly
5. (Optional but powerful) Tiny sparkle
Very light:

2–3 particles near the button or row

fade quickly

👉 adds delight without being childish

⚠️ What NOT to do
❌ no big animations

❌ no confetti

❌ no long delays

❌ no blocking UI

This is feedback, not a moment.

⚙️ Prompt for coding agent
MICRO-INTERACTIONS FOR "FIX" ACTION

Goal:
Provide instant, subtle feedback when user taps "Fix".

---

1. HAPTIC FEEDBACK

On tap:
- trigger light impact haptic

(Expo: Haptics.impactAsync(Haptics.ImpactFeedbackStyle.Light))

---

2. BUTTON ANIMATION

On tap:
- scale button to 0.95 → back to 1.0 (spring or ease-out)
- change label from "Fix" → "Done ✓"

Duration: ~150ms

---

3. ROW HIGHLIGHT

Animate background of the row:
- slight glow / brighter tint (accent color)
- fade in 120ms → fade out 200ms

---

4. MICRO CONFIRMATION TEXT

Show below the row:
- fade in quickly
- visible for ~1.5s
- fade out

Examples:
- "Hook now hits instantly"
- "Clips feel smoother"

---

5. OPTIONAL PARTICLE EFFECT (LIGHT)

- 2–3 small sparkle dots
- appear near button
- fade quickly (<300ms)

---

6. NON-BLOCKING

- user can continue immediately
- no loading states unless needed

---

UX PRINCIPLE

Feedback should feel:
instant, light, satisfying

NOT distracting or slow.

This isn’t just polish.

👉 This is what makes users feel:

“oh… that actually improved my video”

Without needing to analyze anything.

Good catch — and this is actually a real product bug / gap, not just UX.

Right now what’s happening is:

👉 You designed onboarding
👉 But you never actually trigger it reliably

🧠 Why you’re not seeing it
Most likely causes (based on your system):

1. It’s tied to “first-time user” state
Probably something like hasSeenOnboarding = true

Stored locally (AsyncStorage / DB)

👉 So when you refresh:

it thinks you already saw it

it never shows again

2. It’s not hooked to behavior triggers
You intended:

“after 2–3 ideas OR first session”

But right now it’s probably:

❌ only on first install
❌ or not wired at all

3. Dev flow hides it
You’re testing by:

refreshing app

not clearing storage

👉 so onboarding never reappears

🎯 What you actually want (correct behavior)
Onboarding should be:

👉 behavior-triggered, not install-triggered

🔥 Correct logic
Show onboarding when:
Condition A (best trigger)
User has seen ≥ 2 ideas
AND has NOT answered onboarding
Condition B (backup)
User opens app for first time
→ show after first batch
Condition C (recovery)
User shows low engagement
→ re-trigger onboarding later
🧠 Important shift
❌ Wrong:

“Show onboarding once”

✅ Right:

“Show onboarding when it becomes useful”

🔥 What you should implement
1. Add explicit state
hasCompletedTasteOnboarding: boolean
ideasViewedCount: number
2. Trigger logic
if (!hasCompletedTasteOnboarding && ideasViewedCount >= 2) {
  showQuickTune()
}
3. Do NOT block user
show as modal / overlay

allow skip

4. Save answers immediately
Each tap:

👉 updates weights directly
(no waiting, no batching)

5. Add dev reset (you already have similar)
Add button:

👉 “Reset onboarding”

⚙️ Prompt for coding agent
ONBOARDING TRIGGER FIX (QUICK TUNE)

Goal:
Ensure onboarding appears at the right moment based on behavior.

---

1. ADD STATE

- hasCompletedTasteOnboarding (boolean)
- ideasViewedCount (number)

---

2. TRACK IDEA VIEWS

Increment ideasViewedCount when:
- user sees an idea card
- OR scrolls through batch

---

3. TRIGGER ONBOARDING

If:
!hasCompletedTasteOnboarding
AND ideasViewedCount >= 2

→ show Quick Tune modal

---

4. ONBOARDING FLOW

3 questions:
- format
- tone
- hook preference

Each answer:
→ immediately updates weights

---

5. ALLOW SKIP

If user skips:
→ do not mark as completed
→ allow retrigger later

---

6. COMPLETE STATE

After finishing:
→ set hasCompletedTasteOnboarding = true

---

7. DEV TOOL

Add:
"Reset onboarding"

→ clears onboarding state

---

UX PRINCIPLE

Show onboarding when user has context,
not immediately on app open.

don't you think if we put save and post under one button some users who just want to save and post later might otherwise?

You’re right to question it—bundling Save + Post into one button can backfire for users who just want to save and leave.

But you also don’t want to kill momentum by adding friction.

🎯 The right move: one primary action + one quiet alternative
Keep the strong push to post, but don’t trap the user.

✅ Recommended layout
🟢 Primary (big button)
Save & Post

subtext: “Save now, post in seconds”

👉 This keeps momentum

⚪ Secondary (small / subtle)
Just save

👉 low emphasis (text button under CTA)

🧠 Why this works
Most users follow the primary path

Some users need control / safety

You avoid:

hesitation

confusion

frustration

⚠️ What NOT to do
❌ Two equal buttons (“Save” vs “Post”) → decision friction

❌ Only “Save & Post” → feels forced

❌ Complex branching

🔥 Even better (small UX trick)
When user taps “Just save”, show:

Saved ✓

Post it later — it’s ready
👉 keeps the “posting intent” alive

🧠 What this gives you

👉 Idea → Film → Improve → Export → Post → Repeat

🚀 Why this is powerful

👉 we can design “post performance feedback → feeds back into your viral pattern memory”

What does this mean? post performance feedback → feeds back into your viral pattern memory

It means turning what happens after the user posts into signals that update your idea engine—so Lumina learns from real outcomes, not just taps in the app.

Right now your memory uses:

yes/no, export, make another → intent signals

This adds:

views, likes, watch time, saves → outcome signals

🧠 In plain terms
“Did this idea actually perform in the real world?”

If yes → do more of that pattern
If no → reduce it / try adjacent patterns

🔁 How the loop works
1) User posts (outside Lumina)
They go to TikTok/IG and publish.

2) We capture quick feedback (lightweight)
When they come back (or via a notification), ask one tap:

“How did it do?”

🔥 Better than usual

👍 About the same

😐 Worse than usual

(Optional later: let them paste stats or connect accounts)

3) Convert to signals
Map that answer (or real metrics) to weights:

Better → +3

Same → +1

Worse → -2

Attach those weights to the idea’s tags:

structure: denial_loop
hookStyle: "the way I..."
emotionalSpike: awkward
format: mini_story
4) Update viral pattern memory
Increase/decrease weights for those tags.

5) Next ideas improve
Future batches lean into what actually worked, not just what felt good.

🎯 What changes in practice
Before:

“User likes this style”

After:

“This style gets results for this user”

That’s a big upgrade.

🧩 Two ways to implement (start simple)
Option A — Self-reported (fast, MVP)
After return:

Posted?

How did it do?
[🔥 Better] [👍 Same] [😐 Worse]
→ updates weights immediately

Pros: zero integration, works today
Cons: subjective

Option B — Real metrics (later)
User connects TikTok/IG (or pastes stats):

views

likes

watch time / completion

Compute a simple score:

score = (likes/views) + completion_rate
→ compare to user’s baseline → assign + / -

Pros: objective
Cons: more build

⚙️ Where it plugs into your engine
You already have:

memory

weights

evolution engine

Just add one more signal source:

post_performance_signal
and treat it like a high-confidence input (stronger than “liked”)

🧠 Important rules
Don’t overreact to one post

smooth over last 3–5 posts

Keep exploration alive

still keep 30–40% exploratory ideas

Normalize per user

“good” means better than their usual, not absolute numbers

🧪 Example
User posts 3 videos:

Idea pattern	Result	Effect
denial_loop	🔥 better	weight ↑↑
expectation_vs...	😐 worse	weight ↓
small_panic	👍 same	slight ↑
Next batch:

more denial_loop

less expectation_vs_reality

some adjacent exploration

🧠 Why this is powerful
Most tools stop at:

“Here’s an idea”

You go further:

“Here’s what actually works for you”

That’s how Lumina becomes:

personalized

performance-aware

hard to replace

4. Show result instantly
This performed better than your usual 🔥

We’ll lean into this style more
🧠 What happens under the hood
Step 1: Compute score
Keep it simple:

engagement_rate = likes / views
Optional:

score = (likes / views) + (watch_time / 100)
Step 2: Compare to baseline
Each user has:

baseline_engagement = average of last 5 posts
Step 3: Convert to signal
if (score > baseline * 1.2) → +3
if (score ~ baseline) → +1
if (score < baseline * 0.8) → -2
Step 4: Update memory
Apply to:

structure

hookStyle

emotionalSpike

format

🧠 Why this is better than self-report
Instead of:

“I think this was good”

You get:

“this actually performed better”

❌ analytics

Send Codex this:

LUMINA PROJECT CONTEXT — INSPECTOR / AUDITOR ROLE

You are joining this project as a code inspector, QA auditor, and build verifier.

Your job is NOT to invent new features.

Your job is to:
- understand the product vision
- audit what has been built
- verify commits
- catch regressions
- protect Phase 1 scope
- ensure implementation matches the intended user experience

---

PRODUCT

App name:
Lumina

Core promise:
3 daily short-form video ideas that feel like the creator, plus a simple flow to help them film, improve, export, and post manually.

Target users:
Micro-creators who post on TikTok, Reels, or Shorts and struggle with “what should I post today?”

Core insight:
Users do NOT struggle with posting tools.
They struggle with deciding what to post.

Lumina is not an automation platform.
Lumina is a decision-removal and creation-confidence tool.

---

CURRENT STAGE

Phase 1 MVP is built and in QA / validation.

We are NOT at concept stage.

Current focus:
- idea quality
- hook quality
- onboarding clarity
- create/upload flow completion
- export/post intent
- retention signals

Do NOT add scope.

---

PHASE 1 CORE LOOP

The intended loop is:

1. User gets 3 ideas
2. User picks one
3. User films or uploads 1–2 clips
4. Lumina gives simple confidence / improvement guidance
5. User exports/saves
6. User posts manually outside Lumina
7. Signals feed back into memory

Success question:
Can a user find one idea they would actually post, create it, export it, and want to make another?

---

WHAT IS OUT OF SCOPE

Do NOT build:
- auto-posting
- scheduling
- monetization
- earnings
- dashboards
- social feeds
- community features
- complex video editor
- timeline UI
- filters/effects
- heavy ML
- vector DB
- autonomous agents
- Stripe / payouts
- analytics dashboards

If any commit adds these, flag it.

---

PRODUCT PRINCIPLES

1. Idea quality > everything else
2. Hooks must feel personal and scroll-stopping
3. Low effort wins
4. User should not have to think
5. Keep the loop fast
6. Quality beats quantity
7. Style/memory should guide, not overfit
8. Mocks are okay only if they do not block the core loop
9. Every screen should push the user toward action
10. No feature should add friction unless it directly improves idea → export → repeat

---

IDEATOR ENGINE

Current ideator philosophy:

Every idea must have:
- trigger
- visible reaction
- clear payoff
- emotional spike
- low-effort execution

Preferred idea shape:
- mini-stories
- reactions
- limited POV

Current format weighting:
- mini_story heavily preferred
- reaction secondary
- POV only if strong

Hooks must:
- sound like a real thought/text
- contain emotion
- contain tension
- be short
- avoid neutral phrasing

Avoid weak hooks like:
- “POV: you…”
- “when you…”
- “reading…”
unless rewritten into something more personal.

Good hook patterns:
- “the way I…”
- “why do I…”
- “I really just…”
- “this is where…”
- “what I say vs what I do”

---

PERSONALIZATION / MEMORY

Built systems include:

1. Taste calibration
Optional quick-tune questions for:
- format preference
- tone
- hook preference
- effort/privacy

2. Viral pattern memory
Tracks lightweight pattern tags:
- structure
- hookStyle
- emotionalSpike
- format

Signals:
- yes/no
- selected
- exported
- make_another_version
- applied_enhancement

Behavior should override stated preferences.

Memory should learn pattern shape, not just topics.

Good:
User likes denial mini-stories with self-callout.

Bad:
User likes coffee, so generate endless coffee ideas.

---

EVOLUTION ENGINE

The engine should:
- reinforce what works
- suppress rejected patterns
- keep some exploration
- avoid repetition
- not overfit

Important:
Exploration should remain controlled, around ~25% for now.
Do NOT increase exploration aggressively unless requested.

Quality override:
Even if an idea matches memory, reject it if hook/payoff is weak.

---

STYLE HINTS

There is a lightweight style-hint layer.

It should:
- shape hook wording
- shape caption tone
- shape emoji/sentence style

It should NOT:
- override idea quality
- change safety rules
- control structure too strongly

Style affects expression, not core idea logic.

---

CAPTION ENGINE

Captions must extend the moment.

Good captions:
- add internal thought
- add contradiction
- add specific detail

Bad captions:
- generic
- reusable for any video
- repeat the hook
- “this is so relatable”
- vague filler

Emoji:
- max 1 emoji
- only if natural

---

CREATE / UPLOAD FLOW

The create flow should be fast.

Current direction:
- “I’m ready to film”
- “Upload video instead”
- Add Clips screen supports:
  - Clip 1
  - Clip 2 optional
- User can film or upload
- max 2 clips
- no editor
- no timeline
- no complexity

Everything should feel like:
“I know exactly what to do.”

---

POST-EXPORT / READY SCREEN

The post-export screen should feel like a finish line, not a report.

Preferred direction:

Header:
“Your video is ready 🎉”
“Looks good. Now go post it.”

Show:
- final video preview only
- confidence strip
- quick boost optional
- watermark toggle
- Save & Post
- Just save
- Make another version
- Back to ideas

Avoid:
- long analysis
- side-by-side by default
- “why this should perform better” blocks
- technical explanations
- friction before posting

---

QUICK BOOST

Quick Boost is optional and lightweight.

Supported improvements:
- smoother flow / stitch two clips
- faster hook / trim slow start
- caption or hook rewrite only if simple

Button:
Fix → Done ✓

Should have:
- light haptic
- small animation
- row glow
- micro confirmation

Do NOT build full editing.

---

SAVE & POST FLOW

Goal:
Help user move from exported video to manual posting.

Important:
Do not build auto-posting.

Flow:
- Save & Post saves video
- platform sheet can guide to TikTok / Instagram / Snapchat / Copy only
- caption can be copied
- user posts manually outside app

Also support:
- Just save

Do not force posting.

---

SCREENS NEEDING SCRUTINY

Watch these carefully:

1. Studio screen
Should feel like creator control center, not abstract “hive/memory” gate.

2. Profile screen
Should show:
- user style
- taste summary
- tune controls
- optional training

Avoid:
- “No Style Twin yet”
- “train first”
- abstract language
- hard gating

3. Quick Tune onboarding
Must trigger reliably:
- after user has seen 2+ ideas
- if not completed
- optional
- can skip
- should visibly refresh ideas after completion

4. Film screen
Should be action-first:
- hook
- “what you do”
- short visual steps
- CTAs
Avoid too much explanation.

---

MOCKS POLICY

Mocks are acceptable only if they do not block the core loop.

Okay to mock:
- earnings
- social features
- advanced editing
- deep style training
- future dashboards

Not okay to mock:
- idea generation
- idea selection
- film/upload flow
- export/save path
- core signals

Core test:
Can user go from idea → clip → preview → save/export?

---

YOUR ROLE AS CODEX

For every commit or PR, review against:

1. Does this preserve Phase 1 scope?
2. Does it make idea → create → export faster or clearer?
3. Does it introduce feature creep?
4. Does it break memory/personalization?
5. Does it add complexity users do not need?
6. Does it leak internal/system language into UI?
7. Does it keep UX simple and action-oriented?
8. Does it preserve existing signals?
9. Does it avoid regressions in core loop?
10. Does it match the product strategy?

---

WHEN REVIEWING CODE

Please check:

- TypeScript correctness
- schema safety
- migration safety
- API compatibility
- null/fallback behavior
- old clients compatibility
- state persistence
- routing
- UI copy
- mobile/web parity
- no accidental scope creep
- no broken core loop

If something is questionable, classify as:
- BLOCKER
- HIGH
- MEDIUM
- LOW
- WATCH ITEM

---

DEFAULT DECISION RULE

If a change helps:
idea → create → export → repeat

It is probably good.

If a change adds:
more decisions, more screens, more explanation, more features

It is probably risky.

---

CURRENT PRIORITY

Do not add new major systems.

Current priority:
1. polish core screens
2. verify onboarding/taste tuning triggers
3. make post-export flow push posting
4. test with real users
5. fix only friction that blocks action

Send this to your coding agent:

SECURE OAUTH FLOW — BACKEND CODE EXCHANGE

Goal:
Move all OAuth client secrets out of the Expo/mobile app and into the backend.

Problem:
Any EXPO_PUBLIC_*_CLIENT_SECRET is exposed in the mobile JS bundle.
Production OAuth secrets must never live in the client.

---

TARGET ARCHITECTURE

Mobile app:
- Holds only public client IDs
- Starts OAuth login
- Receives authorization code
- Sends code to backend

Backend:
- Holds client secrets in process.env
- Exchanges authorization code for access token
- Stores tokens securely
- Refreshes tokens when needed

---

FLOW

1. Mobile starts OAuth

App opens platform auth URL with:
- client_id
- redirect_uri
- response_type=code
- scope
- state

No client_secret in mobile.

---

2. Platform redirects back

Mobile receives:

authorization_code
state

---

3. Mobile sends code to backend

POST /api/oauth/:platform/exchange

Body:
{
  code,
  redirectUri,
  state
}

---

4. Backend exchanges code

Backend uses env vars:

TIKTOK_CLIENT_SECRET
INSTAGRAM_CLIENT_SECRET
YOUTUBE_CLIENT_SECRET

Backend sends request to platform token endpoint.

---

5. Backend stores tokens

Store:
- access_token
- refresh_token if provided
- expires_at
- platform_user_id
- scopes
- connected_at

Associate with authenticated Lumina user.

---

6. Backend returns safe response

Return only:

{
  connected: true,
  platform: "tiktok",
  username/displayName if available
}

Do NOT return raw access tokens to mobile unless absolutely required.

---

ENV VARIABLES

Add server-only env vars:

TIKTOK_CLIENT_ID
TIKTOK_CLIENT_SECRET
TIKTOK_REDIRECT_URI

INSTAGRAM_CLIENT_ID
INSTAGRAM_CLIENT_SECRET
INSTAGRAM_REDIRECT_URI

YOUTUBE_CLIENT_ID
YOUTUBE_CLIENT_SECRET
YOUTUBE_REDIRECT_URI

Remove production use of:

EXPO_PUBLIC_*_CLIENT_SECRET

Client may keep only:

EXPO_PUBLIC_TIKTOK_CLIENT_ID
EXPO_PUBLIC_INSTAGRAM_CLIENT_ID
EXPO_PUBLIC_YOUTUBE_CLIENT_ID

---

SECURITY REQUIREMENTS

1. Validate state parameter
- prevent CSRF
- state should be generated before OAuth start
- stored temporarily server-side or signed

2. Validate redirectUri
- only allow approved redirect URIs

3. Never log tokens or secrets

4. Encrypt stored tokens if possible

5. Add token refresh logic later

6. Add disconnect endpoint:

DELETE /api/oauth/:platform

Removes stored tokens for user.

---

API ROUTES

Add:

POST /api/oauth/:platform/exchange
GET /api/oauth/:platform/status
DELETE /api/oauth/:platform

Supported platforms:
- tiktok
- instagram
- youtube

---

DATABASE

Add table if needed:

connected_accounts {
  id
  user_id
  platform
  platform_user_id
  display_name
  access_token_encrypted
  refresh_token_encrypted
  scopes
  expires_at
  connected_at
  updated_at
}

Unique:
(user_id, platform)

---

IMPORTANT

Do NOT build auto-posting yet.

This is only secure account connection plumbing for future production use.

No publishing.
No scheduling.
No analytics dashboard.

---

SUCCESS CRITERION

Production OAuth secrets are backend-only.

The mobile app can connect accounts without exposing client secrets.
Key rule: mobile starts OAuth, backend finishes OAuth.

- sound like a text message
- be short
- contain tension
- feel personal
- avoid neutral phrasing

- “when you…”
- “reading…”
unless unusually strong.

- “the way I…”
- “why do I…”
- “I really just…”
- “what I say vs what I do”

Caption must extend the moment.

Good:
- internal thought
- contradiction
- specific detail

Bad:
- generic filler
- repeats hook
- applies to any video

Max 1 emoji.

What about doing but with different wording, like the first example don’t keep saying ‘the way I’ every time

Yes — this is a very important refinement, and you’re thinking in exactly the right direction.

If you keep repeating:

“the way I…”

👉 your app will feel formulaic and boring very fast

👉 topic

👉 hook phrasing

🔥 What you want

expectation vs reality (I said vs what actually happened)

You should generate multiple hook styles

🎯 Example (your exact case)
Base idea (structure)
👉 “I said I’d go to bed early but it’s 3am”

❌ Bad (what you want to avoid)
the way I said I’d go to bed early and it’s 3am  
the way I said I’d go to the gym and I didn’t  
the way I said I’d save money and I didn’t
👉 repetitive → feels AI-generated

✅ Good (same idea, different phrasing)
Style 1 — “the way I…”
the way I said I’d go to bed early and it’s 3am
Style 2 — “why do I…”
why do I say I’ll sleep early like I don’t know myself
Style 3 — internal realization
I really just said I’d sleep early and it’s 3am
Style 4 — contrast
me saying I’ll sleep early vs me at 3am
Style 5 — moment-based
that moment you realize it’s 3am and you said you’d sleep early
Style 6 — curiosity
this is where my “sleep early” plan went wrong
👉 SAME IDEA
👉 COMPLETELY different feel

1. Structure (pattern)
expectation vs reality

denial

overthinking

small panic

2. Hook style (expression)
the_way_i

why_do_i

i_really_just

contrast

moment

curiosity

👉 hook style rotates
👉 app feels fresh

why do I keep opening the fridge like something changed

this is where I opened the fridge again for no reason
👉 feels completely different
👉 same underlying behavior

["the_way_i", "why_do_i", "i_really_just", "contrast", "moment", "curiosity"]

Idea 1 → style A

Idea 2 → style B

Idea 3 → style C

Step 3 — avoid repetition
Rule:

👉 don’t use same hook style twice in a batch

AI still:

crafts best wording

but you guide style selection

feel natural

preserve tension

What if I use Llama 3.2 Vision (the 11B version, quantized down to 4-bit or 8-bit)

Thought for a couple of seconds
You can, but I would not make Llama 3.2 Vision 11B the main ideator.

Best use:

Use Llama 3.2 Vision as a local style/video understanding helper, not as the creative brain.

Where it makes sense
Use it for:

describing uploaded videos

detecting visible style: talking head, POV, reaction, outfit, food, room, face close-up

extracting simple visual cues

helping build the Style Profile

checking if a video has a clear reaction moment

Example output:

{
  "contentType": "reaction",
  "setting": "bedroom",
  "energy": "low",
  "visualStyle": "casual talking-head",
  "reactionVisible": true,
  "privacyRisk": false
}
That would be valuable.

Where I would NOT use it
Do not rely on it for:

final idea generation

hook writing

caption writing

taste/personality matching

viral pattern ranking

Your current prompt-based ideator is tuned very specifically. A smaller local vision model may produce more generic, less sharp hooks.

4-bit / 8-bit reality
8-bit
Better quality, heavier runtime.

4-bit
Cheaper/faster, but more likely to:

miss nuance

hallucinate visual details

produce weaker descriptions

struggle with subtle humor/style

For Lumina, subtlety matters. If the model misses “awkward low-energy self-aware humor,” your personalization gets worse.

My recommendation
Use this architecture:

Uploaded video
→ Llama Vision extracts visual/style hints
→ lightweight JSON Style Profile
→ your main ideator uses that profile
→ final ideas/hooks/captions generated by stronger text model
So:

Llama Vision = eyes
Ideator = brain

Best Phase 1 use case
Use it only for style extraction, not generation.

Good first task:

Analyze this video and return:
- setting
- content type
- energy level
- visual action
- reaction clarity
- privacy risk
- suggested format
Then feed that into your existing memory/style system.

Final verdict
Yes, it can help cut costs and improve personalization, but only if used narrowly.

Do not replace your ideator with it yet. Use it as a local visual style extractor.

}

generic

weak hook

no tension

unclear payoff

If needed:

👉 or rewrite locally with rules

Now:
Llama local → near $0

occasional refinement → $0.005–$0.01

your system should pick the good ones

LLAMA 3.1 IDEATOR INTEGRATION

If you want next:

👉 I can help you design the exact filtering/scoring system so Llama outputs feel as sharp as GPT-level hooks


Yes design that

Here’s the Llama 3.1 filtering/scoring system you should use.

The goal is simple:

Let Llama generate cheaply, but never let weak ideas reach the user.

Send this to your coding agent:

LLAMA IDEATOR — QUALITY FILTER + SCORING SYSTEM

Goal:
Use Llama 3.1 as a low-cost candidate generator, then filter aggressively so only strong, post-worthy ideas reach the user.

Do NOT rely on Llama judgment alone.
Llama generates candidates.
Our scoring system selects winners.

---

FLOW

1. Llama generates 7–10 candidate ideas.
2. Each candidate must include:
   - hook
   - whatToFilm
   - howToFilm
   - caption
   - structure
   - hookStyle
   - emotionalSpike
   - format
3. Run deterministic scoring/filtering.
4. Keep top 3 only.
5. If fewer than 3 pass, generate another candidate batch OR fall back to cached idea pool.
6. Never show weak filler just to hit count.

---

HARD REJECT FILTERS

Reject immediately if:

1. Hook starts with weak neutral phrasing:
- "POV: you..."
- "when you..."
- "watching..."
- "reading..."
- "talk about..."
- "share your thoughts..."

Exception:
Only allow if hook has strong tension and feels personal.

2. Idea is abstract:
- advice
- motivation
- general statement
- personality trait
- vague concept

3. No visible action:
User cannot film it with face/body/phone/object reaction.

4. No tension:
Nothing is wrong, contradictory, awkward, ironic, or unexpected.

5. Private/sensitive content:
- bank apps
- real DMs/messages
- medical info
- salary
- personal documents
- addresses
- IDs
- passwords

6. Too much effort:
- needs props
- needs location change
- needs other people
- needs complex editing

7. Caption is generic:
- “this is so me”
- “too real”
- “relatable”
- repeats the hook
- could apply to any video

---

SCORING

Score each surviving candidate from 0–10.

1. Hook impact — 0 to 2
0 = plain/descriptive
1 = decent
2 = scroll-stopping / makes you pause

2. Tension — 0 to 2
0 = no conflict
1 = mild conflict
2 = clear contradiction, regret, denial, panic, embarrassment, irony

3. Filmability — 0 to 2
0 = requires effort/setup
1 = doable
2 = can film immediately in one location

4. Personal fit — 0 to 2
0 = generic / not creator’s style
1 = somewhat fits
2 = sounds like this creator would say it

5. Caption strength — 0 to 1
0 = generic/repeats hook
1 = extends the moment with thought, contradiction, or specific detail

6. Freshness — 0 to 1
0 = stale/overused
1 = familiar but fresh angle

Total = 10

---

PASS THRESHOLDS

- 8–10: keep
- 6–7: rewrite hook/caption once, then rescore
- <6: reject

If hook score is 0:
reject, even if total is high.

If tension score is 0:
reject.

If filmability score is 0:
reject.

---

REWRITE RULE

For candidates scoring 6–7:

Rewrite only:
- hook
- caption

Do NOT change the entire idea unless necessary.

Hook rewrite must:
- sound like a real thought
- include tension
- avoid neutral phrasing
- be short
- feel personal

Caption rewrite must:
- add internal thought OR contradiction OR specific detail
- not repeat the hook
- max 1 emoji

If rewrite does not raise score to 8+:
reject.

---

BATCH SELECTION

Select top 3 ideas.

Batch rules:
1. No duplicate hookStyle across all 3 if possible.
2. No duplicate scenario family if possible.
3. At least 2 ideas should match user memory.
4. 1 idea may be adjacent exploration.
5. Mini-story preferred if user memory supports it.
6. Quality override always wins.

If diversity conflicts with quality:
choose quality.

---

LOCAL FALLBACK IDEA POOL

If fewer than 3 pass after 2 Llama attempts:

Use cached high-quality idea templates.

Fallback templates must already have:
- approved hook pattern
- approved caption pattern
- pattern tags

Do NOT return weak Llama ideas.

---

OUTPUT

Return exactly 3 ideas if possible.

Each returned idea must include:
{
  hook,
  whatToFilm,
  howToFilm,
  caption,
  whyThisWorks,
  patternTags: {
    structure,
    hookStyle,
    emotionalSpike,
    format
  }
}

---

QA

Test with:
1. Dry/self-aware creator
2. Chaotic creator
3. New user with no memory

For each:
- generate 10 candidates
- log rejected count
- log final selected 3
- verify no weak neutral hooks pass
- verify captions are not filler

Success:
Llama can be cheap, but visible output still feels premium.
The key rule is: Llama is allowed to be messy internally. The user should only ever see the winners.

“why do I…”

“what I say vs what I do…”

👉 This is your template engine

swap topic

swap context

slight wording shifts

👉 This multiplies ideas for free

generates new patterns

fills gaps

adds freshness
