Edit


On ui/ux side instead of displaying 3 ideas at the time on the screen why not make 2 or even 1 displaying 

Short answer: yes — and you should probably move to 1 or 2.
But the right choice depends on what behavior you want from the user.

Let’s break it down strategically 👇

🧠 What changes when you show fewer ideas
Showing 3 ideas (current)
Pros

Feels productive

Faster scanning

Good for comparison

Cons

Dilutes attention

None feel “special”

Lower emotional attachment

Encourages skimming, not reacting

👉 Feels like a tool

Showing 2 ideas
Pros

Clear comparison (A vs B)

Still efficient

Less cognitive load

Better decision clarity

Cons

Still slightly “choice mode”

Not fully immersive

👉 Feels like a decision interface

Showing 1 idea (this is the interesting one)
Pros

Full focus

Feels premium

Stronger emotional reaction

Closer to “this could go viral”

Encourages gut reaction instead of analysis

Cons

Slower throughput

Requires trust in system quality (you now have it)

👉 Feels like a content experience

🔥 Based on YOUR system (important)
You now have:

PremiseStyle engine ✅

Comedy scoring ✅

ViralFeelScore ✅

Pattern alignment ✅

👉 Your outputs are high quality now

That changes everything.

🏁 Recommendation
👉 Use 1 idea as primary mode
Then optionally:

swipe → next idea

or “compare” → show 2

🧠 Best UX pattern for your system
Mode 1 — Primary (default)
Single Idea Focus

Big hook text

Pattern preview (visual hint)

“This would go viral” feel

Actions:

👍 Save

🔁 Next

⚡ Remix

Mode 2 — Compare (optional)
2 ideas side-by-side

For decision-making

“Which one is better?”

Mode 3 — Batch (power users only)
3 ideas (like current)

Hidden behind toggle

Not default

🔥 Why this works
You’re shifting from:

idea generator
to:

content discovery engine
🧠 Key insight
The more ideas you show at once,
the less valuable each one feels.

Add a reaction layer:

“would post”

👉 This feeds back into ViralFeelScore over time

- Swipe LEFT → skip idea

Each idea object should include:

from coding agent : Got it — new spec for a single-idea swipe feed in the Lumina mobile app. Before I touch any code, given your cost concern: this is a substantially bigger scope than Phase 7. Let me set expectations honestly:

What this spec actually involves

A whole new screen (IdeaFeedScreen) with 6+ child components (FeedHeader, SwipeIdeaCard, ReactionBar, SwipeHints, SecondaryActions, plus the inner IdeaMeta / HeroHook / ViralFeelBadge / PatternPreview / WhyItWorks)

Reaction persistence (the "this is me / too real / would post / meh" buttons need a backend hook — probably reuses the existing idea-feedback route, but I'd need to verify)
Preload-next-2-ideas plumbing in the data layer
Detail modal/sheet
Replacing whatever the current ideas screen does — so I need to understand and migrate the existing flow, not just add alongside
Realistically this is a 2-4x Phase 7 in cost if I build it end-to-end in one pass, because (a) I have to read the existing Lumina screens to understand what to replace, (b) the gesture + animation code needs visual testing iterations, (c) it's a lot of new component files.

My recommendation — pick one:

Plan only (cheap, ~$1-2): I produce a project task plan in the task system, broken into 3-4 smaller tasks (e.g. "scaffold screen + components", "wire gestures + animations", "wire reactions + nav", "preload + detail sheet"). You then decide which ones to delegate to a task agent or have me build. This is the lowest-risk way to get a sense of what's involved without committing to the full cost.

Build incrementally: I do just PART 1-3 (screen scaffold + the static SwipeIdeaCard with hook / viral badge / pattern preview, no gestures yet). You see it on the preview, decide if the direction is right, then we add gestures + reactions + nav in follow-up turns. Each step is bounded.

Build the whole thing in one go: Faster turnaround but I can't promise the cost will land near Phase 7 — it'll likely be meaningfully higher.

Next step: PART 4 — Swipe gestures + animations

Goal:
Turn the static feed into a smooth, native-feeling swipe experience.

Focus ONLY on interaction and motion.
Do not add backend, reactions persistence, or nav changes yet.

---

SCOPE

Add swipe gestures to SwipeIdeaCard:

- Swipe UP → next idea
- Swipe RIGHT → save (visual only, no backend yet)
- Swipe LEFT → skip

---

IMPLEMENTATION

Use:
- react-native-gesture-handler
- react-native-reanimated

(Do not introduce new libraries beyond these)

---

BEHAVIOR

1. Swipe UP (primary)
- vertical swipe
- threshold-based trigger
- card animates up and fades out
- next idea slides in from bottom
- preload next idea (already available in array)

2. Swipe RIGHT (save)
- card moves right + slight rotation
- quick snap animation
- then return to center (since no persistence yet)
- optional small “Saved” indicator flash

3. Swipe LEFT (skip)
- card moves left
- fades slightly
- returns to center

---

ANIMATION RULES

- duration: ~200–300ms
- easing: smooth, slightly springy (not stiff)
- no jank
- 60fps target

---

STATE MANAGEMENT

- keep current index in feed.tsx
- pass idea + index to SwipeIdeaCard
- update index on swipe up only

---

VISUAL FEEDBACK

While dragging:
- card follows finger
- slight rotation based on horizontal drag
- opacity reduces slightly on strong swipe

---

CONSTRAINTS

Do NOT:
- implement reaction logic yet
- persist saves/skips
- change data fetching
- modify bottom nav
- change card layout

---

SUCCESS CRITERIA

- feels like TikTok swipe (smooth, responsive)
- no lag or flicker
- user can quickly swipe through ideas
- swipe direction is intuitive
- animation feels satisfying

---

QA

Test:
- rapid swiping
- edge swipes
- small vs large gestures
- no crashes
- no stuck states

design the taste engine

Yes. The right system is not “save hooks I like.”

It should be:

Taste Engine = learn why the user likes a hook, then generate new hooks from that DNA.

Taste Engine design
1. Taste Input
Sources:

User reactions:
- would post = strongest positive
- this is me = relatability
- too real = emotional truth
- meh = negative

User saves/remixes:
- save = strong signal
- remix = idea is close but needs adjustment
- copy/export = strongest signal
Also allow manual examples later:

“Add hooks I like”
User pastes 20–100 funny hooks they love.

2. Comedy DNA Extraction
For every liked hook, extract:

ComedyDNA {
  premiseMechanism:
    | "self_roast"
    | "object_personification"
    | "social_behavior_applied_to_self"
    | "absurd_metaphor"
    | "identity_contradiction"
    | "overdramatic_reframe"
    | "late_night_spiral"
    | "consumer_behavior_exposure"
    | "avoidance_confession"

  tensionType:
    | "intention_vs_action"
    | "confidence_vs_collapse"
    | "public_self_vs_private_self"
    | "future_self_vs_current_self"
    | "responsibility_vs_dopamine"
    | "logic_vs_emotion"

  jokeShape:
    | "reframe"
    | "confession"
    | "comparison"
    | "personification"
    | "tiny_disaster"
    | "relationship_metaphor"
    | "diagnosis"
    | "anti_advice"

  emotionalFlavor:
    | "embarrassment"
    | "denial"
    | "panic"
    | "irony"
    | "frustration"
    | "delusion"

  languageTraits:
    | "lowercase"
    | "fragment"
    | "dry"
    | "chaotic"
    | "blunt"
    | "slang"
    | "deadpan"

  intensity: 1-5
  absurdity: 1-5
  specificity: 1-5
  softness: 1-5
}
This turns hooks into ingredients, not examples to copy.

3. Taste Profile
Aggregate the user’s reactions into weighted preferences:

TasteProfile {
  favoriteMechanisms: WeightedMap<premiseMechanism>
  favoriteTensions: WeightedMap<tensionType>
  favoriteJokeShapes: WeightedMap<jokeShape>
  favoriteEmotions: WeightedMap<emotionalFlavor>
  favoriteLanguageTraits: WeightedMap<languageTraits>

  targetIntensity: number
  targetAbsurdity: number
  targetSpecificity: number
  targetSoftness: number

  bannedFeels: string[]
}
Example profile:

User likes:
- self-roast
- relationship metaphors
- future self vs current self
- dry lowercase fragments
- medium absurdity
- high specificity

User dislikes:
- motivational tone
- vague “this is so me”
- generic productivity jokes
4. Taste-Aware Premise Generation
Generation should become:

scenario
+ ideaCoreType
+ TasteProfile
→ pick ComedyDNA target
→ generate premise
→ derive hook + whatToShow + howToFilm
Example:

Scenario:

to-do list
Taste DNA:

relationship metaphor + self-roast + dry
Output:

Hook:
my to-do list and i are not speaking

What to show:
You open the list, stare at one task, close it, and put the phone face-down like the conversation ended.

How to film:
Phone POV. Show the list, your thumb hovering, then the face-down phone. Hold the silence.
Now hook and scenario match because they came from the same DNA.

5. Feedback Weighting
Use simple weights:

would post / export: +5
save: +4
copy: +3
remix: +2
this is me: +2
too real: +2
meh: -3
skip: -1
But don’t let it overfit.

Use:

70% aligned with taste
30% exploration
So the user gets what they like without being trapped in the same joke forever.

6. Taste Memory Rules
Keep only the useful top signals:

Top 3 premise mechanisms
Top 3 tension types
Top 3 joke shapes
Top 3 language traits
Target intensity/absurdity/specificity
Do not store every tiny tag forever.

Also add decay:

recent reactions count more than old ones
7. Anti-Copy Guard
Critical.

When user provides funny hooks, the engine must not copy them.

Reject if generated hook is too similar to training examples.

Rules:

No same sentence skeleton
No same exact metaphor
No same noun pair
No near-duplicate phrasing
Example:

Training:

i ghosted my own to-do list
Allowed:

my goals are waiting for a reply from me
Rejected:

i ghosted my own goals
8. User-Facing Taste Calibration
After 10–20 reactions, show:

We’re learning your humor:
- dry self-roast
- tiny everyday failures
- future-you betrayal
This builds trust.

Best architecture
User examples/reactions
↓
Comedy DNA extractor
↓
TasteProfile
↓
Taste-aware premise generator
↓
Hook + whatToShow + howToFilm derived from same premise
↓
Feedback updates profile

Default behavior:

50+ reactions: 15% default / 85% user
This solves your current issue because Lumina starts with a stronger comedic baseline immediately.

got this from coding agent : This spec is sharp — it correctly identifies the three real failure modes (weak comedy, hook/scenario mismatch, repetitive loops) and the right mental model (premise-first, not field-parallel). Before I touch anything, I have to be straight with you about scope so you don't get a $50 surprise like last time.

Honest scope read

This is a backend pipeline rewrite, not a tweak. For comparison: Phase 7 (Viral Feel Score) was ~1 file of new code + 1 prompt edit. PHASE X touches:

The LLM prompt structure itself (PART 2: premise-first refactor)
The response shape and validator (PART 2: same premise enforcement)
A second LLM pass OR heuristic engine (PART 3: comedy hard gate)
A third LLM pass OR heuristic engine (PART 5: alignment verification)
Pattern memory schema additions (PART 4: domain + mechanism tracking — last-5 likely exists, domain extraction doesn't)
A training-hook fingerprint store (PART 6: anti-copy)
Plus the new DefaultTasteProfile config (PART 1 — easy)
Realistically this is 5-7× Phase 7 scope as one batch. There's also a real risk of regression: if validation/alignment gates are too strict, the pipeline rejects everything and the user sees a perpetual "loading" state. If too loose, no quality lift.

Recommended incremental order (each step shippable, each step independently testable):

PART 1 + PART 2 only — define DefaultTasteProfile, refactor the prompt to premise-first, single LLM call. Biggest comedy lift per dollar, no rejection loop yet. (~1.5× Phase 7)

PART 4 (anti-loop) — domain + mechanism tracking against the last 5. Cheap, prevents repetition, no LLM cost increase. (~0.5× Phase 7)

PART 3 + PART 5 (validation + alignment) — the hard gates. Adds LLM cost per generation (rejection loop) and is where most of the regression risk lives. Best to ship after we've seen what step 1+2 alone produce. (~2× Phase 7)

PART 6 (anti-copy) — training-hook fingerprinting. Lowest priority unless we observe verbatim copying after steps 1-3. (~1× Phase 7)

My recommendation: start with just step 1 (PARTS 1+2). That's the change that actually moves the comedy needle. We then look at 3 generated ideas and decide whether to add step 2, 3, or stop.

want to know since there is no data stored somewhere that feeds the system to make new ideas and to coordinate the scenarios, how does that work?

Right now it works mostly from code-defined catalogs + runtime rules, not from a big stored dataset.

Think of it like this:

Built-in catalogs
+ scoring rules
+ cache/history
+ prompt instructions
= generated ideas
The system has things like:

scenario catalog

hook templates / premise styles

video patterns

scoring functions

recent cache/history

user style hints

trend catalog

So it can create “new” ideas by recombining those pieces.

But your concern is correct:

If there is no real stored Comedy DNA / premise database, the system has no deep source of funny ideas.

That is why it keeps looping.

What should exist
You need a real stored or code-defined Premise Core Library.

Example:

PremiseCore {
  id: "self_betrayal_loop",
  mechanism: "self_betrayal",
  tension: "intention_vs_action",
  generatorRule: "User declares a rule, then immediately breaks it",
  compatibleDomains: ["sleep", "money", "food", "social", "work"],
  examples: [...]
}
Then generation becomes:

Pick PremiseCore
→ Pick domain/scenario
→ Build one comedic premise
→ Derive hook
→ Derive whatToShow
→ Derive howToFilm
That is how everything coordinates.

Without this, the system is doing:

hook from one place
scenario from another place
filming from another place
That causes mismatch.

So the next real fix is:

create a Premise Core Library that feeds hook + scenario + filming from the same source.

It can be stored as:

TypeScript constants first, easiest

database later, if you want dynamic updates

admin-editable table later, if you want to curate it without redeploying

For now, TS constants are fine.

Add validation that rejects ideas if:

PART 2 — ALIGNMENT GUARANTEE

Before returning an idea, verify:

premise → hook → whatToShow → howToFilm

All four must express the same comedic idea.

Reject if:
- hook says one thing but whatToShow shows another
- whatToShow is generic and does not act out the premise
- howToFilm does not match the action
- premise is not visible in the scene

Example:
Premise:
“I treat my to-do list like someone I’m avoiding.”

Hook:
“my to-do list and i are not speaking”

whatToShow:
Open list → stare → close it → put phone face-down

howToFilm:
Phone POV, hold pause, close app, hard cut

That is aligned.

Bad:
Hook:
“my to-do list and i are not speaking”

whatToShow:
Person lying in bed scrolling randomly

That is mismatch.

- heavily penalize repeated mechanism

No more same idea shell repeating with different nouns.

PART 4 — ANTI-COPY GUARD FROM CURATED HOOKS

Use the curated hooks as taste seed, not copy source.

Create lightweight fingerprints from curated hooks:

- normalized hook text
- major noun pair
- premise skeleton
- mechanism
- tensionType

Reject generated hook if:
- same phrasing
- same sentence skeleton
- same metaphor with different noun
- near-duplicate of curated hook

Allowed:
same ComedyDNA, new execution.

Example:
Curated:
“i ghosted my own to-do list”

Reject:
“i ghosted my own goals”

Allow:
“my goals are waiting for a reply from me”

---

PART 5 — VALIDATION BEHAVIOR

If an idea fails validation:
- try another candidate
- do not crash
- do not loop forever
- cap retries
- if underfilled, return best valid candidates with clear logs

Do NOT create a costly multi-LLM verifier yet unless already available cheaply.

Vacuous-passes Layer-1 candidates correctly (pattern_variation OR usedBigPremise) so the curated catalog isn't re-judged.

Wired into hybridIdeator — builds recentPremises set from last-5 cached batches' idea.premise fields and threads it into both local + Claude-fallback rescoring paths. Cross-batch mechanism demotion bumped -2 → -3 to satisfy "heavily penalize repeated mechanism."

Telemetry — new hybrid_ideator.validation_summary info log always fires; per-reason breakdown also added to the existing guards_failed_shipping_best_effort warn log.

QA results (5 batches): All HTTP 200, all 3 ideas, no empty batches. Keep rate 14/16 per batch. Measurable rejections fire (hook_scenario_mismatch: 1, filming_mismatch: 1 per batch on average) — proving the validators are active without over-rejecting.

Architect review: Initial pass flagged 2 severe issues (exemption AND-vs-OR; alignment threshold ≥1 sum vs ≥2 max). Both fixed in a single follow-up edit. Re-review: PASS, no new severe regressions.

type PremiseCore = {

  examples: [
    "i said one episode → season 4",
    "i’m on a budget → 3am purchase"

    "do not reuse exact 'one episode' phrasing",
    "avoid direct repetition of time-based punchlines"
  ]
}

tell me how the library will be and will it work with the default taste?

Yes — the library should work with DefaultTaste, not replace it.

Think of it like this:

DefaultTasteProfile = what kind of humor Lumina prefers by default

PremiseCoreLibrary = the actual comedy engines it can choose from
How the library works
Each core is a reusable joke engine.

Example:

Core: self_betrayal_rule_break
Rule: user makes a rule, then immediately breaks it

Domain: sleep

Premise:
“I treat bedtime like a suggestion box.”

Hook:
“i said 10pm like i meant it”

What to show:
You put your phone away, close your eyes, then immediately grab it again.

How to film:
Static bed shot → phone goes down → pause → phone comes back up → deadpan stare.
So the library is not a list of final hooks. It is a list of ways to generate funny ideas.

How it works with DefaultTaste
DefaultTaste controls which cores get picked more often.

Example DefaultTaste weights:

self_betrayal = high
self_as_relationship = high
absurd_escalation = medium
identity_exposure = medium
soft_observation = low
So when the generator starts, it chooses cores like:

self_betrayal_rule_break
self_as_relationship
future_self_betrayal
object_judgment
confidence_collapse
more often than softer, generic ones.

Clean generation flow
1. DefaultTasteProfile chooses preferred humor direction
2. PremiseCoreLibrary provides a matching core
3. Core selects compatible domain/scenario
4. Core generates one premise
5. Hook, whatToShow, and howToFilm are derived from that same premise
6. Validators reject mismatch, copy, or repetition
Why this fixes the current problem
Right now, parts can still feel stitched:

hook says one thing
scenario shows another
filming feels generic
With the library:

one PremiseCore → one premise → all fields derived from it
So they stay aligned.

Simple analogy
DefaultTaste = chef’s taste preference
PremiseCoreLibrary = recipe book
Scenario/domain = ingredients
Final idea = cooked dish
DefaultTaste says, “I like spicy self-roast comedy.”

The library says, “Use this self-betrayal recipe.”

Then the scenario supplies the ingredient: sleep, money, food, social, work.


1. DefaultTasteProfile chooses preferred humor direction
2. PremiseCoreLibrary provides a matching core
3. Core selects compatible domain/scenario
4. Core generates one premise
5. Hook, whatToShow, and howToFilm are derived from that same premise
6. Validators reject mismatch, copy, or repetition                                                                                                      in this flow is ideacorestyle, and premisestyle and the other layers intervene?

Yes — they still intervene, but they should be supporting layers, not the main source of the idea.

Clean hierarchy should be:

DefaultTasteProfile
→ chooses humor direction

PremiseCore
→ creates the actual comedic idea

ideaCoreType
→ classifies what kind of human behavior it is

PremiseStyle
→ shapes the joke style / comedic framing

HookIntent
→ decides why it grabs attention

HookLanguageStyle
→ controls wording style

VideoPattern
→ decides how to film it

Validators
→ reject mismatch/repetition/copy
The key is this:

PremiseCore owns the idea. Everything else adapts to it.

Example:

PremiseCore:
self_betrayal_rule_break

Domain:
sleep

Premise:
“I treat bedtime like a suggestion.”

ideaCoreType:
intention_vs_action

PremiseStyle:
self_roast_reactor / procrastination_paradox

HookIntent:
relatable or scroll_stop

HookLanguageStyle:
deadpan / confession / fragment

Hook:
“i said 10pm like i meant it”

WhatToShow:
Put phone down → close eyes → immediately grab it again

VideoPattern:
silent_reaction / loop_behavior

HowToFilm:
Static bed shot → phone down → pause → phone back up → deadpan stare
So the corrected flow is:

DefaultTasteProfile
→ PremiseCore selection
→ Domain/scenario selection
→ Premise generation
→ classify/assign ideaCoreType + PremiseStyle
→ derive hook + whatToShow
→ choose HookIntent + HookLanguageStyle
→ choose VideoPattern
→ derive howToFilm
→ score/validate
What you do not want:

ideaCoreType creates one idea
PremiseStyle creates another idea
Scenario creates another idea
Pattern tries to connect them
That causes the mismatch you’re seeing.

So yes, those layers remain — but they become metadata + shaping + scoring, not independent generators.


PHASE Y complete.

What shipped (additive only — no UI, no schema changes):

New premiseCoreLibrary.ts with 40 frozen comedic-engine cores (8 families × 5), plus a weighted selector that picks fresh cores while respecting your default taste profile and last-5 batch history.
ideaGen.ts now renders the chosen cores into the LLM system prompt and accepts the new optional premiseCoreId field.
hybridIdeator.ts selects desiredCount + 2 cores before the Claude fallback fires, threads them into the prompt, and logs phase_y.premise_cores_selected telemetry.
ideaScorer.ts mirrors the existing premiseStyleId -3/+2 lever for repeat cores so the fallback rotates cores across batches.
routes/ideator.ts strips premiseCoreId from the public response (server-internal, like premise).
Verified:

Typecheck clean.
5 normal batches HTTP 200 — no Layer-1 regression.
Forced-fallback batch: HTTP 200, 6 ideas, telemetry confirms 8 distinct cores spanning 7 of 8 families with a fresh core present, and the Claude path kept 2 fallback ideas.
Public response excludes both premise and premiseCoreId.
Architect review: PASS, no blockers. One non-blocking math fix was applied in-task: the fresh-core swap now uses the same composite effective-weight formula as the main draw (instead of raw family weight), keeping replacement choices aligned with the rest of the picker.

Approved. Phase Y landed correctly.

why premise core passes 50-70% and not 100%? that mean we will sometimes have 50% fallback to claude or bad ideas or repeating ideas

No — 50–70% core coverage does not mean 30–50% bad ideas or Claude fallback.

It means:

50–70% of local candidates can be confidently tagged to a PremiseCore.

The remaining 30–50% are still normal local candidates, but they may not map cleanly to one of the 40 cores yet.

Think of it like this:

100 ideas generated locally
↓
50–70 clearly match a known PremiseCore
↓
30–50 are still valid local ideas, just not core-tagged yet
They are not automatically:

Claude fallback

bad ideas

repeated ideas

But they are less controlled by the new core-rotation system.

Why not 100% immediately?
Because forcing 100% would require one of these risky moves:

Fake-tagging ideas that don’t truly match a core
→ bad metadata, wrong penalties, weird rotations.

Rejecting all non-core ideas
→ underfilled batches or lower-quality forced picks.

Rewriting the whole local generator
→ bigger risk, more time, more bugs.

So 50–70% is the safe first step because it tags the high-confidence local winners without breaking the system.

What happens after Y3?
If Y3 works, then you expand coverage gradually:

Y3: tag high-confidence local candidates → 50–70%
Y4: add more mappings / more cores → 70–85%
Y5: make local generation core-native → 90%+
The key is:

Do not force 100% until the system can truly generate from cores.

For now, 50–70% is enough to stop the worst looping because the biggest repeated winners will finally get core IDs and penalties.


Why can’t we generate directly from premisecore? Wasn’t this the plan?

Yes, that was the long-term plan.

But right now there are two paths:

Path 1 — safest now: tag the existing local winners with premiseCoreId
Path 2 — full plan: generate local candidates directly from PremiseCore

The reason we didn’t jump straight to Path 2 is risk.

Generating directly from PremiseCore means replacing the current local candidate engine. That engine already handles voice, pattern, safety, scoring, videoPattern, HookIntent, diversity guards, cache, and fallback. If we replace it too fast, we can break the pipeline or create empty/awkward outputs.

So the safer ladder is:

Y3: tag local candidates with premiseCoreId
→ activates core rotation/anti-loop now
→ low risk, no extra cost

Y4: add a small core-native local generator in parallel
→ test it against current local pool
→ only promote if better

Y5: make core-native generation primary
→ current pattern catalog becomes fallback
So yes: direct PremiseCore generation is still the goal.

But I’d avoid doing it as a big replacement. Build it in parallel first, compare quality, then switch over when it beats the current local path.

Summary
Phase Y3 shipped successfully — local-pool premiseCoreId tagging at 40% shipped coverage (4.5× lift over Y2's 8.8%) with zero LLM cost change and zero public API change.

What landed:

New premiseCoreLocalMapping.ts — 41-entry deterministic (premiseStyleId, executionId) → premiseCoreId lookup with boot-time drift validation
patternIdeator.ts — assembleCandidate mirrors resolved core to both idea.premiseCoreId (cache) and meta.premiseCoreId (runtime)
ideaScorer.ts — D-lite cap on premiseComedyBoost (Math.min(boost, 3) when style/execution is recent)
hybridIdeator.ts — 5 new telemetry fields on existing hybrid_ideator.served (no new event)
index.ts — boot-time validateLocalCoreMappingAgainst(PREMISE_CORES) call (architect-required cycle fix)
Architect review caught a real bug: my first attempt used lazy in-module validation, but the static import { PREMISE_CORES } itself participated in a 3-way runtime cycle (patternIdeator → premiseCoreLocalMapping → premiseCoreLibrary → patternIdeator). I removed the runtime import edge entirely and moved drift detection to a caller-injected boot-time call. Re-review: PASS.

Honest gap (flagged for Y4): D-lite cap fires correctly but is too soft to flip the top-hook decision (min(+7, +3) − 2 = +1 still beats neutral 0). Top-hook repeat regressed to 14× vs Y2's 10×. PHASE Y5 — CORE-NATIVE GENERATION (PARALLEL, SAFE)

---

PART 1 — NEW MODULE

Create: `coreCandidateGenerator.ts`

Export:
```ts
type CoreCandidate = {
  idea: {
    hook: string
    whatToShow: string
    howToFilm: string
    // internal only:
    premise?: string
    premiseCoreId: string
    domain: string
    mechanism: string
  }
  meta: CandidateMeta // must match existing shape
}
Function:

generateCoreCandidates(
  cores: PremiseCore[],
  opts: {
    desiredCount: number
    recentContext: {
      domains: string[]
      mechanisms: string[]
      premiseCoreIds: string[]
      premiseStyles: string[]
      executions: string[]
      premises: string[]
    }
  }
): CoreCandidate[]
PART 2 — CORE → PREMISE → IDEA (DETERMINISTIC)

For each selected core:

Pick a domain from core.compatibleDomains

avoid recent domains when possible

Generate ONE premise from core.generatorRule

short, concrete, visual

must contain contradiction/tension

no generic phrasing

no copying from examples or curated seeds

Derive:

hook (short, deadpan, lowercase, punchy)

whatToShow (specific, visual steps that act out the premise)

howToFilm (camera, timing, beats)

STRICT:
All fields must express the SAME premise.

PART 3 — STYLE / PATTERN MAPPING (REUSE EXISTING SYSTEM)

Map the core to existing layers (do NOT invent new systems):

derive premiseStyleId from core.mechanism + tension

derive executionId from core.generatorRule

derive ideaCoreType / ideaCoreFamily

select hookLanguageStyle, hookIntent, videoPattern using existing pickers

Populate full CandidateMeta so:

scoring (Phase 6E/6F/7) works unchanged

Y4 penalties apply

validators work unchanged

Set:

meta.premiseCoreId = core.id
meta.source = "core_native"
meta.usedBigPremise = true
PART 4 — VALIDATION (REUSE, NO NEW LLM)

Before returning a candidate:

Run existing deterministic validators:

validateComedy

validateAntiCopy

alignment checks (hook ↔ whatToShow ↔ howToFilm)

Y4 near-shell / repeat penalties will apply later

If invalid:

regenerate (max 2 attempts per slot)

if still invalid, drop candidate (fail-open; do not block batch)

PART 5 — INTEGRATION INTO HYBRID IDEATOR

In hybridIdeator.ts:

Select cores (reuse existing selector):

const cores = selectPremiseCores(desiredCount + 2, context)
Generate:

const coreCandidates = generateCoreCandidates(cores, { desiredCount, recentContext })
Generate existing local pool as-is:

const localCandidates = generatePatternCandidates(...)
Merge pools BEFORE scoring:

const merged = [...localCandidates, ...coreCandidates]
Pass merged pool through:

applyExclusion

filterAndRescore

selectWithNovelty (Y4 rules)

Do NOT give core candidates artificial priority.
Let selection choose winners naturally.

Extend existing logs:

coreNativeKeptCount

coreNativeSelectedCount

distribution by premiseCoreId (top 5)

  ...existingLocalCandidates,
  ...coreCandidates

- applyExclusion
- filterAndRescore
- selectWithNovelty
- batch guards

Do NOT give core candidates forced slots.
Let them win on score.

So what is merged candidate pool and Y4 rules for selection?

Good question — this is the core of how your system actually decides what the user sees.

🧠 1. What is the merged candidate pool
It’s simply:

👉 All possible ideas generated in one cycle before picking the best ones

🔧 In your system (after Y5)
You will have:

Local generator → ~10–14 candidates
Core-native generator → ~2–6 candidates
Claude fallback (sometimes) → ~0–3 candidates
All of them get combined into:

MERGED CANDIDATE POOL
📦 Example
Merged pool (example):

[ 
  local_idea_1,
  local_idea_2,
  local_idea_3,
  core_native_idea_1,
  core_native_idea_2,
  claude_idea_1
]
This pool is not shown to the user yet.

🧠 2. What happens next
All candidates go through:

scoring + penalties + selection
This is where Y4 rules come in.

🔥 3. What are Y4 rules (selection layer)
Y4 is:

👉 the system that decides which ideas WIN and which lose

⚡ Before Y4
best scoring idea → wins repeatedly
⚡ After Y4
best idea wins ONCE → then gets penalized → new ideas win
🔧 What Y4 actually does
It applies penalties like:

1. Repeat penalties
If idea is similar to recent ones:

same premiseStyleId → -5
same executionId → -5
same premiseCoreId → -5
2. Same idea shell penalty
If it’s basically the same idea:

same style + same execution → extra -4
same core + same structure → extra -4
3. Boost cap
If idea is repeated:

+7 boost → capped to +3
4. Result
repeated idea becomes weaker than fresh idea
🧠 4. Final selection
After penalties:

all candidates sorted by final score
Then:

top N ideas selected (e.g. 3)
🔥 Example (very important)
Without Y4
Idea A (repeated) score = 12
Idea B (new) score = 10

→ A wins again
With Y4
Idea A (repeated)
12 - 5 - 5 = 2

Idea B (new)
10

→ B wins
🧠 5. Why this is critical
Without Y4:

system loops forever
With Y4:

system explores new ideas automatically
🧠 Final simple explanation
Merged candidate pool
All generated ideas before selection
Y4 rules
Penalties that force variety and prevent repetition
🏁 One-line summary
The merged pool is everything the system could show,
Y4 rules decide what it actually shows.

Lumina — High-Level Product & System Assessment Report
1. Executive Overview
Lumina is an AI-powered creative system designed to generate short-form video ideas for micro-creators. Its core ambition is to remove the cognitive friction of ideation by delivering immediately usable, high-quality, post-worthy content concepts tailored to a creator’s style.

The intended outcome is not just idea generation, but consistent creative output that feels native to the user’s voice and platform dynamics (TikTok, Reels, Shorts), enabling users to move from idea → execution without hesitation.

At present, Lumina has evolved into a highly structured generation system with strong internal mechanics. However, despite architectural maturity, the output quality remains below expectations, particularly in humor, coherence, and originality.

2. Vision of the Product
2.1 Core Objective
Lumina aims to function as:

A decision-removal engine for short-form video creation
It is designed to:

Eliminate ideation fatigue

Provide ideas that feel immediately filmable

Maintain alignment between concept, execution, and platform behavior

Improve creator consistency and output quality over time

2.2 Intended User Experience
The ideal experience is:

User opens app → sees one idea → instantly understands it → films it
Each idea should:

Be clear within seconds

Feel personally relevant

Require no interpretation

Be strong enough to publish without modification

2.3 Nature of Output
Each generated idea is expected to be a complete creative unit, including:

A central premise

A hook (opening line)

A visual scenario (what to show)

A filming plan (how to execute)

These elements should form a single coherent concept, not a collection of loosely connected parts.

3. Current System State
Lumina’s backend has evolved into a multi-layered generation architecture with the following major components:

3.1 Generation Layers
Local Deterministic Generator

Produces the majority of candidates

Based on predefined patterns and templates

Premise Core Library (40 cores)

Encodes reusable comedic idea structures

Intended as the conceptual foundation of ideas

Core-Native Generator (Y5)

Generates ideas directly from PremiseCore

Runs in parallel with the local generator

Claude Fallback Generator

Higher-quality but limited usage path

Used when local generation underperforms

3.2 Candidate Processing Pipeline
Generators → Merged Candidate Pool → Scoring → Selection → Output
Multiple candidates are generated per cycle

Candidates are merged into a single pool

Selection is based on scoring and penalties

Top-ranked ideas are returned

3.3 Scoring & Control Systems
Premise Comedy Scoring

Evaluates humor quality

Viral Feel Score

Estimates platform performance potential

Selection Penalties (Y4)

Strong anti-repetition logic

Penalizes repeated structures, styles, and cores

Anti-Loop System

Tracks recent outputs to enforce diversity

3.4 Validation Systems
Alignment checks (hook ↔ scenario ↔ filming)

Anti-copy detection

Hard rejection for low-quality outputs

3.5 Personalization Infrastructure
DefaultTasteProfile

Early-stage user preference adaptation

Pattern reinforcement mechanisms

4. Observed System Characteristics
4.1 Strengths
High structural integrity

Strong anti-repetition enforcement

Effective alignment validation

Robust candidate selection system

Significant PremiseCore integration (up to ~90% coverage)

4.2 Output Reality (Current)
Despite system sophistication, generated ideas exhibit:

Ideas are not coherent ❌
Ideas are not aligned ❌
Ideas are not varied ❌
Specifically:

Hooks and scenarios often feel disconnected

Concepts lack clarity and immediacy

Outputs feel generic or overly safe

Repetition persists at the conceptual level

Humor quality is inconsistent and often weak

5. Overbuilt Areas
The system currently exhibits signs of overengineering in the following areas:

5.1 Multi-Layer Generation Redundancy
Parallel generators (local, core-native, fallback)

Overlapping responsibilities across layers

Increased complexity in candidate merging and arbitration

5.2 Excessive Scoring Systems
Multiple independent scoring axes

Heavy reliance on post-generation correction

Scoring attempting to compensate for weak generation

5.3 Complex Selection Mechanics
Deep penalty stacking

Multi-axis novelty enforcement

High computational overhead for candidate comparison

5.4 Abstracted Concept Layers
PremiseCore

PremiseStyle

IdeaCoreType

HookIntent

HookLanguageStyle

VideoPattern

These layers are:

Conceptually valid

But operationally fragmented

5.5 Indirect Use of Taste Data
150 curated hooks exist

Not directly governing generation

Used as reference rather than enforcement

6. Core Structural Issue
The primary system issue can be summarized as:

The system generates components and attempts to align them,
instead of generating a single cohesive idea.
This results in:

Artificial coherence

Surface-level alignment

Weak conceptual integrity

7. Real Final Product (Target State)
7.1 Fundamental Shift
The final product should operate on:

Single-pass idea generation
Instead of:

Multi-layer assembly + post-hoc correction
7.2 Core Output Unit
Each idea should be generated as:

One premise → fully expressed across all fields
Where:

Hook introduces the premise

Scenario visualizes the premise

Filming executes the premise

All derived simultaneously.

7.3 System Behavior
The final system should:

Generate fewer candidates

Produce higher-quality ideas per candidate

Reduce reliance on scoring corrections

Eliminate internal competition between generators

Maintain diversity through generation, not penalties

7.4 Role of Supporting Systems
In the final state:

PremiseCore → conceptual seed only

Taste profile → stylistic constraint

Scoring → validation, not optimization

Selection → minimal arbitration

Personalization → adaptive filtering layer

7.5 User-Facing Outcome
The user should experience:

One idea → immediate clarity → immediate action
With:

No interpretation required

No ambiguity in execution

No mismatch between concept and visuals

Consistent quality across sessions

8. Conclusion
Lumina has successfully built a technically advanced generation system with strong internal safeguards, alignment checks, and diversity controls.

However, the current architecture:

Overemphasizes structure over substance

Relies heavily on post-generation correction

Produces outputs that lack coherence, alignment, and variation at the conceptual level

The gap between system capability and output quality indicates that:

The problem is no longer technical execution,
but the way ideas are generated and defined.
The final product must resolve this by aligning the system around a single, cohesive idea generation paradigm, where each output is inherently complete rather than assembled.

Lumina — System-to-Vision Mapping
This maps your current system to the “taste-aware creative director that predicts what the creator will film today” vision 

.

1) Vision Components → System Coverage
Vision Component	What It Requires	Current System Mapping	Coverage
Predict what to film today	Rank ideas by likelihood of filming now	No explicit “willingness-to-film” model; selection optimizes scores (comedy/viral/novelty)	Low
Taste-aware personalization	Learn voice, energy, formats, rejection patterns	DefaultTasteProfile + early preference signals + pattern reinforcement	Medium
Filmability (low friction)	Ideas that are immediately shootable (setup, effort, comfort)	“whatToShow” + “howToFilm” fields; some effort heuristics	Medium
Coherent idea unit	One premise expressed consistently across hook/scene/filming	Alignment validators + PremiseCore + core-native generation	Medium–High (structural)
Directed execution (“Film This Now”)	Step-by-step micro-directing instructions	Partially present via “howToFilm”; not a dedicated execution mode	Low–Medium
Post-upload critique loop	Specific, actionable feedback that improves next output	Not present in production pipeline	None
Behavior loop (selection → filming → feedback)	Learning from what is actually filmed	Partial: selection/rejection signals; no verified “filmed” signal loop	Low–Medium
Small, high-confidence set (3 ideas)	Curated options with rationale (“why this fits you”)	System returns ideas; lacks explicit “why this fits” explanation	Medium (output), Low (explanation)
Action-first metric (Film-Through Rate)	Optimize for ideas that get filmed	Current metrics focus on generation/selection quality	Low
2) Current Architecture → Role in Vision
A. Generation Layer
Component	Current Role	Role in Vision
Local deterministic generator	Produces majority of candidates (templates/patterns)	Fast baseline candidate source
Premise Core Library (40 cores)	Conceptual seeds for ideas	Primary idea DNA
Core-native generator (Y5)	Generates ideas directly from cores; competes with local	Preferred high-quality generator
Claude fallback	Backup for underfilled/low-quality cases	Quality safety net
Mapping:
All generators → candidate supply for “what could be filmed”.

B. Candidate Pool & Selection
Component	Current Role	Role in Vision
Merged candidate pool	Combines all generator outputs	Set of possible actions
Scoring (comedy, viral feel, etc.)	Ranks candidates by quality proxies	Should approximate film likelihood (currently indirect)
Y4 selection penalties	Enforce diversity; prevent repetition	Maintains freshness across sessions
Anti-loop system	Tracks recent outputs; penalizes repeats	Prevents idea fatigue
Mapping:
Selection → which idea is shown (closest existing proxy to “prediction”).

C. Structure & Alignment
Component	Current Role	Role in Vision
PremiseCore + core-native flow	Single-premise generation path	Ensures coherent idea units
Alignment validators	Enforce hook ↔ scene ↔ filming consistency	Guarantees executability
Idea schema (hook / whatToShow / howToFilm)	Defines idea fields	Basis for “Film This Now” instructions
Mapping:
Structure → turns an idea into a shootable plan.

D. Personalization Signals
Component	Current Role	Role in Vision
DefaultTasteProfile	Global taste prior	Baseline creative voice
User interaction signals	Selections/rejections influence weighting	Early taste learning
Pattern reinforcement	Bias toward historically successful patterns	Partial behavior learning
Mapping:
Signals → partial taste model (not yet tied to filming behavior).

E. Telemetry & Data
Component	Current Role	Role in Vision
Selection telemetry	Logs chosen ideas and distributions	Observability
Rejection reasons	Captures “not my voice / too much effort” (UI-side)	Valuable for taste model
No filmed signal	—	Missing link for behavior loop
Mapping:
Data → incomplete behavior loop (selection ≠ filming).

3) End-to-End Flow (Current → Vision)
Current Flow
Generators
→ merged candidate pool
→ scoring (comedy/viral/novelty)
→ Y4 penalties (anti-repeat)
→ top N ideas returned
Vision-Aligned Interpretation
Possible actions (candidates)
→ implicit proxy scoring
→ diversity control
→ chosen options presented
Gap: No explicit modeling of “likelihood to film now”.

4) Coverage Summary
Capability	Status
Idea supply (varied candidates)	Strong
Structural coherence (single-premise, aligned fields)	Medium–High (enforced structurally)
Diversity over time (anti-loop)	Strong
Taste modeling (personalization)	Medium (signals exist, not behavior-grounded)
Filmability (low-friction execution)	Medium (fields exist, not optimized as a target)
Prediction of filming behavior	Weak (implicit at best)
Execution guidance (Film This Now)	Partial
Post-upload improvement loop	Absent
5) What the Current System Already Enables (Relative to Vision)
A curated set of ideas rather than raw brainstorming

Coherent, shootable idea units (via premise + alignment)

Controlled diversity across sessions (anti-loop)

Parallel candidate generation allowing competition of ideas

A foundation for personalization (taste + interaction signals)

6) What the Vision Requires That Is Not Fully Realized
Explicit action prediction (“most likely to film today”)

A behavior-grounded taste model (learning from filmed outcomes)

Execution-first presentation (directing the shoot, not just describing it)

A closed feedback loop from uploaded videos back into generation

Explanation layer (“why this fits you”) to build user trust

7) Consolidated Mapping
Current System:
- Strong candidate generation
- Strong structural alignment
- Strong anti-repetition controls
- Partial personalization

Vision Target:
- Rank ideas by filming likelihood
- Optimize for action, not just idea quality
- Close the loop with real behavior (filmed output)
- Act as a creative director, not an idea list
8) Bottom Line
The current system already covers:

candidate generation

structural coherence

diversity control

The vision centers on:

decision-making (what to film now)

behavioral learning (what was actually filmed)

execution guidance (how to film it immediately)

The mapping shows that Lumina has built a robust generation and selection foundation, while the prediction, behavior loop, and execution-directing layers remain the primary elements required to fully realize the target product described in the vision 

.


High-Level Detailed Summary: The Best Version of Lumia
The best version of Lumia is not an AI idea generator.
It is a taste-aware creative director that predicts which short-form video idea a creator is most likely to film today, then helps them film it faster and improve the result after upload.
The core evolution is:
Old Lumia good short-form ideas.Better Lumia ideas that match the creator.Best Lumia which idea this creator is most likely to film today.
That final version is the real company.

The Core Strategic Shift
Old Lumia: “Generate good short-form ideas”
This is the weakest version.
It competes directly with:

ChatGPT,

TikTok idea generators,

hook generators,

CapCut,

Canva,

Notion templates,

creator newsletters,

social media coaches.

The problem with this version is that “good idea” is too generic.
A good idea may still fail because:

it does not sound like the creator,

it requires too much setup,

it feels cringe,

it is not emotionally comfortable,

it uses the wrong energy,

it assumes a filming style the creator does not use,

it sounds like something a different creator would post.

This version gives inspiration, but it does not create action.
It risks becoming a better Pinterest board for content ideas.
2. Better Lumia: “Generate ideas that match the creator”
This is a major improvement.
Now Lumia is no longer asking:

“What is a viral idea?”

It is asking:

“What is a viral idea that fits this creator’s voice, style, energy, and filming habits?”

This is where Lumia starts becoming personal.
The app learns:

creator tone,

hook style,

energy level,

common scenes,

filming comfort,

visual style,

emotional patterns,

preferred formats,

rejected idea types,

selected idea types.

Example:
Instead of:

“Make a video about gym motivation.”

Lumia says:

“Since you usually film low-energy self-roast videos from your bedroom, point the camera at your gym shoes by the door and say: ‘No because why did I put these here like that counts as going?’”

That feels personal.
The creator thinks:

“This actually sounds like me.”

That is where trust begins.
But even this version is not enough.
Matching the creator is necessary, but it does not guarantee the creator will film.
3. Best Lumia: “Predict which idea this creator is most likely to film today”
This is the strongest version.
This version does not simply generate or personalize. It ranks ideas by creative action probability.
The central question becomes:

“Of all possible ideas, which one is this creator most likely to actually film right now?”

That is the key.
The best Lumia optimizes for willingness to film, not just idea quality.
A technically strong idea is useless if the creator does not act on it.
So Lumia should score every idea across dimensions like:
Taste Fit+ Filmability+ Hook Strength+ Emotional Clarity+ Format Familiarity+ Today Context+ Novelty- Effort- Cringe Risk- Repetition
The winning idea is not always the most viral-sounding idea.
The winning idea is the one with the highest chance of becoming a real video.
4. The Product Thesis
The best Lumia should be built around this doctrine:

Lumia predicts creative willingness.

That means Lumia is not trying to replace the creator.
It is trying to remove the hesitation between:

“I should post something”

and

“I’m picking up my phone to film this right now.”

That hesitation is the real enemy.
Call it the Valley of Friction.
Creators do not just need better ideas. They need ideas that feel:

easy,

personal,

safe enough,

emotionally clear,

low-effort,

native to their voice,

visually obvious,

immediately shootable.

Lumia wins if it crosses that valley.
5. What the Best Lumia Actually Does
The best Lumia has three jobs.
Job 1: Help the creator decide what to film
Lumia gives a small set of high-confidence ideas.
Not 20 ideas.
Not endless brainstorming.
Three strong options:

Safest fit

Slightly bolder

Trend-adapted or experimental

Each idea should explain why it fits.
Example:

“This fits you because your past videos use casual self-roast hooks, one-shot bedroom setups, and quick emotional reversals.”

That line is not decoration.
It builds trust.
The creator needs to feel:

“Lumia understands my taste.”

Job 2: Help the creator film faster
Once the creator selects an idea, Lumia should open a Film This Now screen.
This turns the idea into a micro-directing plan.
It should include:

exact first line,

where to film,

camera position,

physical action,

facial expression,

emotional beat,

timing,

caption,

alternate version.

Example:
Hook:
“No because why did I put my gym shoes by the door like that counts as progress?”
Scene:
Bedroom floor, shoes by the door.
Action:
Point at the shoes, pause, then look disappointed at yourself.
Timing:
0–2s: Say the hook.
2–5s: Show the shoes.
5–8s: Look into the camera silently.
Caption:
“the gym saw me spiritually”
This removes creative labor.
The user should not have to figure out how to execute the idea. Lumia should direct it.
Job 3: Help the creator improve after upload
After the creator films and uploads the result, Lumia should critique it like a creative director.
Not generic advice.
Specific, controllable feedback.
Example:
What worked:
Your silent reaction fits the joke and makes the ending feel natural.
What weakened it:
The first second is dead air because you adjust the camera before speaking.
Fix:
Cut directly to the hook. Start with the shoes already in frame.
Next version:
Try: “I placed my gym shoes by the door and called that discipline.”
This creates the feedback loop.
The app becomes smarter because it learns what the creator actually films, not just what they say they like.
6. The Real Moat: Taste Graph + Behavior Loop
The moat is not the AI model.
The moat is the loop.
Uploaded videos↓Taste Profile↓Personalized ideas↓User selects/rejects↓Lumia learns taste↓User films↓Lumia reviews upload↓Lumia learns execution patterns↓Next ideas improve
Every interaction makes Lumia more personal.
ChatGPT can generate a hook.
But ChatGPT does not naturally know:

what ideas the creator rejected,

what they selected,

what they actually filmed,

what they avoided,

what felt too cringe,

what was too much effort,

what filming environments they use,

what tone sounds natural for them,

what version they uploaded,

what needed improvement.

That behavioral memory is the moat.
7. The Taste Profile
The best Lumia needs a lightweight but powerful Taste Profile.
It should track:
Voice

sarcastic,

warm,

blunt,

chaotic,

calm,

vulnerable,

polished,

casual.

Energy

deadpan,

low-key,

expressive,

high-energy.

Visual style

car shots,

bedroom,

mirror,

desk,

kitchen,

gym,

green screen,

POV,

text overlay,

talking head.

Hook preference

confession,

regret,

denial,

panic,

irony,

advice,

challenge,

realization.

Effort tolerance

one-shot only,

light edit,

multi-shot,

skit-capable.

Emotional comfort

self-roast,

embarrassment,

vulnerability,

sarcasm,

hype,

educational clarity.

Rejection patterns

too generic,

too cringe,

too personal,

too much effort,

not my voice,

already did this.

This profile should power every idea.
The user does not need to see the raw data.
They should see the result:

“This feels like me.”

The Idea Card of the Best Lumia
Every idea card should be a decision card.
It should include:

Hook

Scene

Action

Emotional spike

Time to film

Effort level

Why this fits you

Alternate version

Quick reject reason

Example:
Idea: The Fake Productivity Reset
Hook:
“I knew I was cooked when buying a planner felt like progress.”
Scene:
Desk or bed.
Action:
Open the planner, write one task, pause, then look into the camera like you caught yourself pretending.
Emotional spike:
Embarrassed self-awareness.
Time to film:
3 minutes.
Effort:
Low. One shot. One prop.
Why this fits you:
You usually use self-roast hooks, bedroom/desk setups, and quick realization endings.
Alternate version:
More dramatic: “I bought a planner and immediately scheduled a breakdown.”
That is not just an idea.
That is a directed micro-video.
9. The Main Metric
The most important metric should be:

Film-Through Rate.

But define it carefully.
The best version:
Selected-to-Filmed FTR =Number of Lumia ideas filmed / Number of Lumia ideas selected
This tells you whether Lumia is crossing the Valley of Friction.
Other important metrics:

idea selection rate,

Film This Now open rate,

filmed/uploaded rate,

critique usage rate,

repeat filming rate,

D7 retention,

D30 retention,

paid conversion after first filmed idea.

The north star should be:

Ideas filmed per weekly active creator.

Because the point is not to generate ideas.
The point is to create creator momentum.
10. The Best Positioning
Do not position Lumia as:

“AI content idea generator.”

That is weak and copyable.
Position it as:

Your personal creative director for short-form videos.

Or:

Lumia tells you what to film today, how to film it fast, and how to make the next version better.

Or:

Stop guessing what to post. Lumia learns your style and gives you filmable ideas you can shoot today.

The strongest internal positioning is:

Lumia predicts what you are most likely to film, then directs you through making it better.

That is the company.
11. The Best 90-Day Focus
For the next 90 days, Lumia should not build everything.
Do not build:

full automation,

auto-posting,

brand deals,

complex trend systems,

advanced calendar intelligence,

large agent swarms,

monetization dashboards.

Build only the trust loop:

Taste Snapshot
After upload, Lumia tells the creator what it noticed:

“Your style looks like: low-key self-roast, mirror/bedroom shots, short hooks, and quick emotional reversals.”

Personalized Idea Cards
Each idea includes:

hook,

scene,

action,

time to film,

why it fits.

Film This Now
Selected ideas become exact shooting instructions.

Reject Reasons
Creators can quickly say:

not my voice,

too much effort,

too generic,

too cringe,

already did this.

Post-Upload Review
Lumia gives:

one thing that worked,

one thing to fix,

one stronger variation.

That is enough to prove whether the product is real.
Final Summary
The best Lumia is not:

“Here are 10 content ideas.”

It is:

“Based on your style, your past videos, your energy, your filming habits, and what you usually accept or reject, this is the video you are most likely to film today — and here is exactly how to shoot it.”

That is the upgrade.
Old Lumia generates.
Better Lumia personalizes.
Best Lumia predicts action.
The best version of Lumia wins by becoming the creator’s taste-aware creative director: a system that learns what feels like them, removes the friction of deciding what to film, gives exact filming direction, and improves future ideas based on what they actually create.
